revision.comment: 15 revisions imported: Bot: import full history from ja:奈良波良神社 up to rev 97989166
revision.text:
{{神社 |名称= 奈良波良神社 |画像=[[File:Narahara-jinja.jpg|270px]] |所在地 = [[三重県]][[度会郡]][[玉城町]]宮古[[小字|字]]矢倉戸833 |緯度度 = 34 | 緯度分 = 28 | 緯度秒 = 18.5 |経度度 = 136 |経度分 = 37 | 経度秒 = 29.5 |地図= Japan Mie |祭神= 那良原比女命 |社格= [[式内社]]（小）
[[皇大神宮]]摂社 |創建= [[延暦]]23年（[[804年]]）以前式内社研究会 編（1990）：237ページ |本殿= [[神明造]] |別名= |札所等= |例祭= |神事= [[神嘗祭]] }} '''奈良波良神社'''（ならはらじんじゃ）は、[[伊勢神宮]][[皇大神宮]]（内宮）の[[摂末社|摂社]]。内宮の摂社27社のうち第22位である宇治山田市役所 編（1929）：9 - 11ページ。 社名の奈良波良は「楢原」を意味し、周辺地域が[[ナラ]]の木の多い原野であったことが窺われる伊勢文化舎 編（2008）：59ページ学研パブリッシング（2013）：67ページ。 == 概要 == [[三重県]][[度会郡]][[玉城町]]宮古[[小字|字]]矢倉戸833に鎮座する辰巳出版（2013）：48ページ。[[丘陵]]地のやや下った地に鎮座し、東西は民有林に囲まれる。[[倭姫命]]が定めた[[神社]]である。石を[[神体]]とする金子（1983）：59ページ。 === 社名と鎮座地 === 社名は「奈良波良神社」であるが、『皇太神宮儀式帳』では「楢原神社」と表記する。社名より、周辺がナラの木の原野であったことが分かるが、鎮座地の宮古は[[豊受大神宮]]（外宮）の[[神田]]である「宮古御園」のあった場所である。 『[[勢陽五鈴遺響]]』は、鎮座地「宮古」の名がこの地に[[遷都]]が予定されたものの[[大仏山公園|大仏山]]が湧出したためにかなわず、[[地名]]だけ[[首都|都]]（＝宮古）になったと解説する。『式内社調査報告』ではこの説を「妄説」と断じているが、社名の「'''[[奈良]]'''波良」と「宮古」（＝都）の関係を指摘している。玉城町役場は、宮古の旧表記は「宮子」であり、神様の恵を受ける宮の子であると解説する{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.tamaki-kanko.jp/html/special_jingu-yukari.html|title=神宮ゆかりの地を巡る|publisher=玉城町役場産業振興課|work=のほほん たまき旅|accessdate=2014-06-01|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20140601033540/http://www.tamaki-kanko.jp/html/special_jingu-yukari.html|archivedate=2014-06-01}}。 == 境内 == 社地の[[面積]]は9,500[[平方メートル|m2]]式内社研究会 編（1990）：239ページ。[[古代]]の社地は5町（≒49,585m2）であった。[[江戸時代]]に再興された後、[[明治4年]]（[[1871年]]）に[[上知令]]で社域林を没収されたものの[[1892年]]（明治25年）に6段8畝19歩（≒6,807m2）が返還された。[[ヒノキ]]を中心とし、[[スギ]]・[[マツ]]なども混交する社叢が形成されている。 [[境内]]には信仰者から寄進された[[自然石]]製の[[手水鉢]]がある。[[紀州藩]]の建立した禁殺生石も残る。 社殿は境内のほぼ中央にあり、[[神明造]]の[[板葺]]で長さ5尺（≒1.5[[メートル|m]]）×広さ3尺5寸（≒1.1m）×高さ9尺7寸（≒2.9m）である式内社研究会 編（1990）：240ページ。南側を向き、1重の[[玉垣]]に囲まれ、[[神明鳥居]]を備える。 == 祭神 == [[祭神]]は那良原比女命（ならはらひめのみこと）。『宇治山田市史』では「奈良波良比女命」と表記する宇治山田市役所 編（1929）：11ページ。大水上命（おおみなかみのみこと）の子である。 地域の[[産土神]]であり、[[天津神・国津神|国津神]]であった。田の灌漑、神田や楢原の[[守護神]]とされる。伝承によれば、倭姫命の旅の苦労を慮った那良原比女命は、倭姫命に守護地を差し出したという。 == 歴史 == 『皇太神宮儀式帳』に「同[[内親王]]定祝」とあることから、倭姫命の創建とされる。同書は[[延暦]]23年（[[804年]]）のものであることから、それ以前には既に存在し、祀られてきたことが分かる。[[中世]]には修繕もままならず、いつの日にか廃絶し、[[寛文]]3年[[9月8日]]（[[グレゴリオ暦]]：[[1663年]][[10月8日]]）に大宮司の大中臣精長（[[河辺精長|河邊精長]]）の尽力により再興された式内社研究会 編（1990）：237, 240ページ。当地の古老が「若宮」という地名を太神宮末社と伝承していたことから、「若宮ノ谷」の西に再興したのであった式内社研究会 編（1990）：238ページ。しかし『皇太神宮儀式帳』に記載された「坐五町、[[四至]]竝大山」と、再興地が一致しないことから、異論が続出している。薗田守良は『神宮典略』で旧社地が宮古の山奥にあったとし、[[御巫清直]]は『二宮管社沿革考』で再興地の字名が「矢倉戸」であることから再興されたのは[[愛洲氏]]の櫓跡であり、本来の社地は[[曹洞宗]]神照山廣泰寺だとした式内社研究会 編（1990）：237 - 238ページ。上都神社を旧社地とする説もある{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.isekanko.jp/k-cycling/course18.html|publisher=伊勢市観光協会|title=コース18 玉城ルート1 〜里山の神社を巡る〜|accessdate=2014-06-01|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20131026234812/http://www.isekanko.jp/k-cycling/course18.html|archivedate=2013-10-26}}。このような異論がある中、再建以降、社地の変遷はない。 社殿は[[1980年]]（昭和55年）[[10月8日]]に造り替えられた。 == 祭祀 == 常駐の[[神職]]はいないが、[[祝部]]が1名任命されており、平時の管理を務めている式内社研究会 編（1990）：238 - 239ページ。 [[祈年祭]]（[[2月19日]]）・[[月次祭]]（[[6月19日]]と[[12月19日]]）・[[神嘗祭]]（[[10月19日]]）・[[新嘗祭]]（11月25日）には[[権禰宜]]、宮掌、出仕らが社前にて[[祭祀]]を行い、[[歳旦祭]]（[[1月1日]]）・[[元始祭]]（[[1月3日]]）・建国記念祭（[[2月11日]]）・風日祈祭（[[5月14日]]と[[8月4日]]）・[[天長祭]]（[[天皇誕生日]]）は内宮の五丈殿で遥祀を行う。 == 周辺 == 宮古集落から西へ進み、広瀬精工玉城工場に隣接する坂を下り、[[砂利道]]に入ると神社がある伊勢文化舎 編（2008）：57ページ。付近には[[古墳]]群がみられる三重県環境生活部文化振興課（2012）：8ページ。 * [[東海旅客鉄道|JR]][[参宮線]][[田丸駅]]より徒歩約35分。 * [[伊勢自動車道]][[玉城インターチェンジ|玉城IC]]から[[自動車]]で約2分（約830m）。 == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist|2}} == 参考文献 == * [[伊勢文化舎]] 編『お伊勢さん125社めぐり』別冊『伊勢人』、伊勢文化舎、平成20年12月23日、151p. ISBN 978-4-900759-37-4 * 宇治山田市役所 編『宇治山田市史 上巻』宇治山田市役所、昭和4年1月20日、862p. * [[学研パブリッシング]]『伊勢神宮に行こう』Gakken Mook神社紀行セレクションvol.1、[[薗田稔]]監修、学研マーケティング、2013年7月4日、82p. ISBN 978-4-05-610047-1 * 金子延夫『玉城町史 第1巻―南伊勢の歴史と伝承―』三重県郷土資料叢書第61集、三重県郷土史料刊行会、昭和58年8月10日、276p. * 式内社研究会 編『式内社調査報告 第六巻 東海道1』[[皇學館大学]]出版部、平成2年2月28日、690p. ISBN 4-87644-080-8 * 三重県環境生活部文化振興課『熊野脇道 三重県 みえ歴史街道ウォーキングマップ』三重県環境生活部文化振興課、2012年 * 『日本人なら知っておきたい 伊勢神宮と125の社』タツミムック、[[辰巳出版]]、2013年11月1日、127p. ISBN 978-4-7778-1197-7 == 関連項目 == * [[伊勢国の式内社一覧]] * [[神宮125社の一覧]] * [[下外城田村]] == 外部リンク == {{ external media | align = | width = 230px | image1 = [http://www.sengukan.jp/wp-content/themes/sengukan/images/shrine/area04/area04_04.jpg 奈良波良神社] }} * [http://www.jingukaikan.jp/125mairi/m08/0803.html 奈良波良神社（ならはらじんじゃ）] - 一般財団法人伊勢神宮崇敬会 * [http://www.town.tamaki.mie.jp/hpdata/kanbun/jinjya.html 神社めぐり] - 玉城町 {{神宮125社}} {{神道 横}} {{デフォルトソート:ならはらしんしや}} [[Category:神宮125社]] [[Category:伊勢国の式内社]] [[Category:摂末社]] [[Category:玉城町の歴史]] [[Category:三重県の神社]] [[Category:玉城町の建築物]]

revision.comment: Immanuelle moved page [[奈良波良神社]] to [[Narahara Shrine]] without leaving a redirect: Bot: merge import
revision.text:
{{神社 |名称= 奈良波良神社 |画像=[[File:Narahara-jinja.jpg|270px]] |所在地 = [[三重県]][[度会郡]][[玉城町]]宮古[[小字|字]]矢倉戸833 |緯度度 = 34 | 緯度分 = 28 | 緯度秒 = 18.5 |経度度 = 136 |経度分 = 37 | 経度秒 = 29.5 |地図= Japan Mie |祭神= 那良原比女命 |社格= [[式内社]]（小）
[[皇大神宮]]摂社 |創建= [[延暦]]23年（[[804年]]）以前式内社研究会 編（1990）：237ページ |本殿= [[神明造]] |別名= |札所等= |例祭= |神事= [[神嘗祭]] }} '''奈良波良神社'''（ならはらじんじゃ）は、[[伊勢神宮]][[皇大神宮]]（内宮）の[[摂末社|摂社]]。内宮の摂社27社のうち第22位である宇治山田市役所 編（1929）：9 - 11ページ。 社名の奈良波良は「楢原」を意味し、周辺地域が[[ナラ]]の木の多い原野であったことが窺われる伊勢文化舎 編（2008）：59ページ学研パブリッシング（2013）：67ページ。 == 概要 == [[三重県]][[度会郡]][[玉城町]]宮古[[小字|字]]矢倉戸833に鎮座する辰巳出版（2013）：48ページ。[[丘陵]]地のやや下った地に鎮座し、東西は民有林に囲まれる。[[倭姫命]]が定めた[[神社]]である。石を[[神体]]とする金子（1983）：59ページ。 === 社名と鎮座地 === 社名は「奈良波良神社」であるが、『皇太神宮儀式帳』では「楢原神社」と表記する。社名より、周辺がナラの木の原野であったことが分かるが、鎮座地の宮古は[[豊受大神宮]]（外宮）の[[神田]]である「宮古御園」のあった場所である。 『[[勢陽五鈴遺響]]』は、鎮座地「宮古」の名がこの地に[[遷都]]が予定されたものの[[大仏山公園|大仏山]]が湧出したためにかなわず、[[地名]]だけ[[首都|都]]（＝宮古）になったと解説する。『式内社調査報告』ではこの説を「妄説」と断じているが、社名の「'''[[奈良]]'''波良」と「宮古」（＝都）の関係を指摘している。玉城町役場は、宮古の旧表記は「宮子」であり、神様の恵を受ける宮の子であると解説する{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.tamaki-kanko.jp/html/special_jingu-yukari.html|title=神宮ゆかりの地を巡る|publisher=玉城町役場産業振興課|work=のほほん たまき旅|accessdate=2014-06-01|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20140601033540/http://www.tamaki-kanko.jp/html/special_jingu-yukari.html|archivedate=2014-06-01}}。 == 境内 == 社地の[[面積]]は9,500[[平方メートル|m2]]式内社研究会 編（1990）：239ページ。[[古代]]の社地は5町（≒49,585m2）であった。[[江戸時代]]に再興された後、[[明治4年]]（[[1871年]]）に[[上知令]]で社域林を没収されたものの[[1892年]]（明治25年）に6段8畝19歩（≒6,807m2）が返還された。[[ヒノキ]]を中心とし、[[スギ]]・[[マツ]]なども混交する社叢が形成されている。 [[境内]]には信仰者から寄進された[[自然石]]製の[[手水鉢]]がある。[[紀州藩]]の建立した禁殺生石も残る。 社殿は境内のほぼ中央にあり、[[神明造]]の[[板葺]]で長さ5尺（≒1.5[[メートル|m]]）×広さ3尺5寸（≒1.1m）×高さ9尺7寸（≒2.9m）である式内社研究会 編（1990）：240ページ。南側を向き、1重の[[玉垣]]に囲まれ、[[神明鳥居]]を備える。 == 祭神 『皇太神宮儀式帳』に「同[[内親王]]定祝」とあることから、倭姫命の創建とされる。同書は[[延暦]]23年（[[804年]]）のものであることから、それ以前には既に存在し、祀られてきたことが分かる。[[中世]]には修繕もままならず、いつの日にか廃絶し、[[寛文]]3年[[9月8日]]（[[グレゴリオ暦]]：[[1663年]][[10月8日]]）に大宮司の大中臣精長（[[河辺精長|河邊精長]]）の尽力により再興された式内社研究会 編（1990）：237, 240ページ。当地の古老が「若宮」という地名を太神宮末社と伝承していたことから、「若宮ノ谷」の西に再興したのであった式内社研究会 編（1990）：238ページ。しかし『皇太神宮儀式帳』に記載された「坐五町、[[四至]]竝大山」と、再興地が一致しないことから、異論が続出している。薗田守良は『神宮典略』で旧社地が宮古の山奥にあったとし、[[御巫清直]]は『二宮管社沿革考』で再興地の字名が「矢倉戸」であることから再興されたのは[[愛洲氏]]の櫓跡であり、本来の社地は[[曹洞宗]]神照山廣泰寺だとした式内社研究会 編（1990）：237 - 238ページ。上都神社を旧社地とする説もある{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.isekanko.jp/k-cycling/course18.html|publisher=伊勢市観光協会|title=コース18 玉城ルート1 〜里山の神社を巡る〜|accessdate=2014-06-01|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20131026234812/http://www.isekanko.jp/k-cycling/course18.html|archivedate=2013-10-26}}。このような異論がある中、再建以降、社地の変遷はない。 社殿は[[1980年]]（昭和55年）[[10月8日]]に造り替えられた。 == 祭祀 == 常駐の[[神職]]はいないが、[[祝部]]が1名任命されており、平時の管理を務めている式内社研究会 編（1990）：238 - 239ページ。 [[祈年祭]]（[[2月19日]]）・[[月次祭]]（[[6月19日]]と[[12月19日]]）・[[神嘗祭]]（[[10月19日]]）・[[新嘗祭]]（11月25日）には[[権禰宜]]、宮掌、出仕らが社前にて[[祭祀]]を行い、[[歳旦祭]]（[[1月1日]]）・[[元始祭]]（[[1月3日]]）・建国記念祭（[[2月11日]]）・風日祈祭（[[5月14日]]と[[8月4日]]）・[[天長祭]]（[[天皇誕生日]]）は内宮の五丈殿で遥祀を行う。 [[伊勢自動車道]][[玉城インターチェンジ|玉城IC]]から[[自動車]]で約2分（約830m）。 == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist|2}} == 参考文献 == * [[伊勢文化舎]] 編『お伊勢さん125社めぐり』別冊『伊勢人』、伊勢文化舎、平成20年12月23日、151p. ISBN 978-4-900759-37-4 * 宇治山田市役所 編『宇治山田市史 上巻』宇治山田市役所、昭和4年1月20日、862p. * [[学研パブリッシング]]『伊勢神宮に行こう』Gakken Mook神社紀行セレクションvol.1、[[薗田稔]]監修、学研マーケティング、2013年7月4日、82p. ISBN 978-4-05-610047-1 * 金子延夫『玉城町史 第1巻―南伊勢の歴史と伝承―』三重県郷土資料叢書第61集、三重県郷土史料刊行会、昭和58年8月10日、276p. * 式内社研究会 編『式内社調査報告 第六巻 東海道1』[[皇學館大学]]出版部、平成2年2月28日、690p. ISBN 4-87644-080-8 * 三重県環境生活部文化振興課『熊野脇道 三重県 みえ歴史街道ウォーキングマップ』三重県環境生活部文化振興課、2012年 * 『日本人なら知っておきたい 伊勢神宮と125の社』タツミムック、[[辰巳出版]]、2013年11月1日、127p. ISBN 978-4-7778-1197-7 == 関連項目 == * [[伊勢国の式内社一覧]] * [[神宮125社の一覧]] * [[下外城田村]] == 外部リンク == {{ external media | align = | width = 230px | image1 = [http://www.sengukan.jp/wp-content/themes/sengukan/images/shrine/area04/area04_04.jpg 奈良波良神社] }} * [http://www.jingukaikan.jp/125mairi/m08/0803.html 奈良波良神社（ならはらじんじゃ）] - 一般財団法人伊勢神宮崇敬会 * [http://www.town.tamaki.mie.jp/hpdata/kanbun/jinjya.html 神社めぐり] - 玉城町 {{神宮125社}} {{神道 横}} {{デフォルトソート:ならはらしんしや}} [[Category:神宮125社]] [[Category:伊勢国の式内社]] [[Category:摂末社]] [[Category:玉城町の歴史]] [[Category:三重県の神社]] [[Category:玉城町の建築物]]

revision.comment: Bot: restore translated version
revision.text:
{{Shrine | Name = {{ill|Narahara Shrine|ja|奈良波良神社}} | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = [[File:Narahara-jinja.jpg|270px]] | location = 833 Yagurado, Miyako, [[Tamaki]], [[Watarai District]], [[Mie Prefecture]], Japan | coordinates = {{coord|34|28|18.5|N|136|37|29.5|E|region:JP|display=inline}} | Map = Japan Mie | deity = {{ill|Narahara-hime-no-mikoto|ja|那良原比女命}} | type = [[Shikinaisha]] (minor Rank), {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮}} auxiliary shrine (*sessha*) | Established = Before [[Enryaku era|Enryaku]] 23 (804 CE)<ref name="sk7">Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): p. 237.</ref> | Main Hall = [[Shinmei-zukuri]] | Other Names = | festival = [[Kannamesai]] (Harvest Festival) | Rituals = }}
'''{{ill|Narahara Shrine|ja|奈良波良神社}}''' (*Narahara Jinja*) is an auxiliary shrine (*sessha*) of {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮}} (the Inner Shrine of the [[Ise Grand Shrine]]), located in [[Tamaki]], [[Mie Prefecture]], Japan. It Ranks 22nd among the auxiliary shrines of the Inner Shrine.<ref>Ujiyamada City Office (1929): pp. 9–11.</ref>
The name "Narahara" means "oak plain," suggesting that the surrounding area was once a plain abundant with [[Nara tree|Nara]] (oak) trees.<ref name="ic">Ise Bunka Publishing (2008): p. 59.</ref><ref name="gk">Gakken Publishing (2013): p. 67.</ref>
== Overview == The shrine is located at Yagurado 833, Miyako, [[Tamaki]], [[Watarai District]], [[Mie Prefecture]], Japan.<ref name="tm">Tatsumi Publishing (2013): p. 48.</ref> It is situated on gently sloping terrain surrounded by privately owned forests to the east and west.<ref name="sk7"/> The shrine was established by {{ill|Yamato-hime-no-mikoto|ja|倭姫命|1=Yamatohime-no-mikoto}}.<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk"/> The sacred object (*shintai*) of the shrine is a stone.<ref>Kaneko (1983): p. 59.</ref>
=== Shrine Name and location === Although officially named '''Narahara Shrine''' (*奈良波良神社*), it is referred to as '''Narahara Shrine''' (*楢原神社*) in the ''Ritual Records of the Imperial Grand Shrine'' (*皇太神宮儀式帳*).<ref name="sk7"/> The name suggests that the area was once covered with oak (*nara*) trees. The shrine is located in Miyako, which was the site of the "Miyako Misono," a sacred rice field (*shinden*) belonging to {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮}} (the Outer Shrine of Ise).<ref name="ic"/>
According to the ''Seiyō Gorin Ikkyō'' (*勢陽五鈴遺響*), the name "Miyako" (meaning "capital") derives from an ancient plan to relocate the capital to this area. However, this plan was abandoned due to the emergence of Mt. Daibutsu (*大仏山*), leaving only the name to reflect the intended capital.<ref name="sk7"/> While the ''Report on the Investigation of Shikinaisha'' dismisses this as a "baseless theory," it acknowledges the connection between the shrine's name "'''Nara'''hara" and "Miyako" (都, meaning "capital").<ref name="sk7"/> The Tamaki Town Office explains that the old spelling of Miyako was "Miyako" (*宮子*), meaning "child of the shrine," symbolizing the blessings received from the deities.<ref>{{Cite web|language=ja|url=http://www.tamaki-kanko.jp/html/special_jingu-yukari.html|title=巡る神宮ゆかりの地 (Exploring Places Connected to the Grand Shrine)|publisher=Tamaki Town Office, Industrial Promotion Division|work=Nohon Tamaki Tabi|accessdate=2014-06-01|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20140601033540/http://www.tamaki-kanko.jp/html/special_jingu-yukari.html|archivedate=2014-06-01}}</ref>
== Grounds == The shrine grounds cover an area of 9,500 square meters.<ref name="sk9">Shikinaisha Research Association, 1990, p. 239.</ref> In ancient times, the grounds spanned approximately 5 chō (≈49,585 m²).<ref name="sk9"/> Although the shrine forest was confiscated during the 1871 [[Uchichiryo Edict]], 6,807 m² were returned in 1892.<ref name="sk9"/> The sacred grove is mainly composed of [[hinoki cypress]] trees, with some [[cedar]] and [[pine]] mixed in.<ref name="sk9"/>
Within the grounds, there is a natural stone [[temizuya]] (water basin) donated by devotees.<ref name="ic"/> A stone monument erected by the [[Kishu Domain]] prohibiting the killing of living beings remains on site.<ref name="gk"/>
The shrine pavilion, located near the center of the grounds, is constructed in the [[Shinmei-zukuri]] style with a [[board roof]]. It measures approximately 1.5 meters in length, 1.1 meters in width, and 2.9 meters in height.<ref name="sk0">Shikinaisha Research Association, 1990, p. 240.</ref> It faces south and is enclosed by a single layer of [[tamagaki]] fence with a [[Shinmei torii]] gate.<ref name="sk0"/>
== Enshrined deity == The enshrined deity is **Narahara Hime-no-Mikoto**.<ref name="ic"/> The deity is referred to as "Narawara Hime-no-Mikoto" in the *Ujiyamada City History*.<ref name="uy">Ujiyamada City Office, 1929, p. 11.</ref> She is considered the daughter of **Ōminakami-no-Mikoto**.<ref name="sk7"/>
Worshipped as the [[Ubusunagami]] (guardian deity) of the region,<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk"/> she is classified as a [[Kunitsukami]] (earthly deity).<ref name="tm"/> She is revered as the protector of rice field irrigation, sacred fields, and Narahara.<ref name="tm"/> According to tradition, she dedicated a sacred area to protect [[Yamato-hime-no-mikoto]] during her arduous journey.<ref name="tm"/>
== History == The shrine is believed to have been founded by Yamato-hime-no-mikoto, as mentioned in the *Kōtaijingu Gishiki-chō*.<ref name="sk7"/> Since this document dates back to 804 (Enryaku 23), the shrine's existence predates this period.<ref name="sk7"/> During the [[Medieval period]], the shrine fell into ruin until it was restored in 1663 (Kanbun 3) through the efforts of **Ōnakatomi Seichō (Kawabe Seichō)**.<ref>Shikinaisha Research Association, 1990, pp. 237, 240.</ref>
There is debate regarding the original shrine site, as the description in the *Kōtaijingu Gishiki-chō* does not align with the current location.<ref name="sk7"/> Some theories suggest the original site was deep in Miyako's mountains,<ref>Shikinaisha Research Association, 1990, pp. 237–238.</ref> while others propose it was at **Jōtō Shrine**.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Course 18: Exploring Satoyama Shrines|url=http://www.isekanko.jp/k-cycling/course18.html|publisher=Ise City Tourism Association|accessdate=2014-06-01}}</ref> Despite these theories, the shrine has remained at its current site since its restoration.<ref name="sk7"/>
The current shrine pavilion was rebuilt on October 8, 1980.<ref name="sk9"/>
== Rituals == Although there is no resident [[Kannushi]] (Shinto priest), one **Ikanushi** is appointed to manage daily affairs.<ref name="sk89">Shikinaisha Research Association, 1990, pp. 238–239.</ref>
Key rituals include: - **Kinensai (February 19)**: Prayer for abundant harvest - **Tsukinamisai (June 19 & December 19)**: Monthly festival - **Kannamesai (October 19)**: Offering of the first fruits to the deity - **Niinamesai (November 25)**: Celebration of the new harvest
Additional remote rituals are performed at the **Gojō-den Hall** of the Inner Shrine for New Year’s Day, National Foundation Day (February 11), and other imperial-related festivals.<ref name="sk89"/>
== Surroundings == The shrine is located west of the Miyako settlement, adjacent to the Hirose Seikō Tamaki Factory. It can be accessed via a gravel path after descending a slope.<ref>Ise Culture Publishing, 2008, p. 57.</ref> Nearby are several [[kofun]] (ancient Kofuns).<ref>Mie Prefecture Culture Promotion Division, 2012, p. 8.</ref> * **JR Sangu Line - Taki Station**: Approximately 35-minute walk<ref name="tm"/>. * **Ise Expressway - Tamaki IC**: About 2 minutes (830 m) by car.
== References ==
{{Reflist|2}}
== 参考文献 == * [[伊勢文化舎]] 編『お伊勢さん125社めぐり』別冊『伊勢人』、伊勢文化舎、平成20th year 12月23日、151p. ISBN 978-4-900759-37-4 * 宇治山田市役所 編『宇治山田市史 上巻』宇治山田市役所、昭和4th year 1月20日、862p. * [[学研パブリッシング]]『伊勢神宮に行こう』Gakken Mook神社紀行セレクションvol.1、[[薗田稔]]監修、学研マーケティング、2013rd year 7月4日、82p. ISBN 978-4-05-610047-1 * 金子延夫『玉城町史 第1巻―南伊勢の歴史と伝承―』三重県郷土資料叢書第61集、三重県郷土史料刊行会、昭和58th year 8月10日、276p. * 式内社研究会 編『式内社調査報告 第六巻 東海道1』[[Kogakkan University]]出版部、平成2nd year 2月28日、690p. ISBN 4-87644-080-8 * 三重県環境生活部文化振興課『熊野脇道 三重県 みえ歴史街道ウォーキングマップ』三重県環境生活部文化振興課、2012nd year * 『日本人なら知っておきたい 伊勢神宮と125の社』タツミムック、[[辰巳出版]]、2013rd year 11月1日、127p. ISBN 978-4-7778-1197-7 == See Also == * {{ill|List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|ja|伊勢国の式内社一覧}} * {{ill|List of 125 Shrines of Ise|ja|神宮125社の一覧|lt=List of 125 Shrines of Ise|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|3=伊勢国の式内社一覧}} * [[下外城田村]]
== External Links == {{ external media | align = | width = 230px | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image1 = [http://www.sengukan.jp/wp-content/themes/sengukan/images/shrine/area04/area04_04.jpg 奈良波良神社] }}
* [http://www.jingukaikan.jp/125mairi/m08/0803.html 奈良波良神社（ならはらじんじゃ）] - 一般財団法人伊勢神宮崇敬会 * [http://www.town.tamaki.mie.jp/hpdata/kanbun/jinjya.html 神社めぐり] - 玉城町

{{Shrine | Name = {{ill|Narahara Shrine|ja|奈良波良神社|12=draft}} | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = [[File:Narahara-jinja.jpg|270px]] | location = 833 Yagurado, Miyako, [[Tamaki]], [[Watarai District]], [[Mie Prefecture]], Japan | coordinates = {{coord|34|28|18.5|N|136|37|29.5|E|region:JP|display=inline}} | Map = Japan Mie | deity = {{ill|Narahara-hime-no-mikoto|ja|那良原比女命|12=draft}} | type = [[Shikinaisha]] (minor Rank), {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|12=draft}} auxiliary shrine (*sessha*) | Established = Before [[Enryaku era|Enryaku]] 23 (804 CE)<ref name="sk7">Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): p. 237.</ref> | Main Hall = [[Shinmei-zukuri]] | Other Names = | festival = [[Kannamesai]] (Harvest Festival) | Rituals = }}
'''{{ill|Narahara Shrine|ja|奈良波良神社|12=draft}}''' (*Narahara Jinja*) is an auxiliary shrine (*sessha*) of {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|12=draft}} (the Inner Shrine of the [[Ise Grand Shrine]]), located in [[Tamaki]], [[Mie Prefecture]], Japan. It Ranks 22nd among the auxiliary shrines of the Inner Shrine.<ref>Ujiyamada City Office (1929): pp. 9–11.</ref>
The name "Narahara" means "oak plain," suggesting that the surrounding area was once a plain abundant with [[Nara tree|Nara]] (oak) trees.<ref name="ic">Ise Bunka Publishing (2008): p. 59.</ref><ref name="gk">Gakken Publishing (2013): p. 67.</ref>
== Overview == The shrine is located at Yagurado 833, Miyako, [[Tamaki]], [[Watarai District]], [[Mie Prefecture]], Japan.<ref name="tm">Tatsumi Publishing (2013): p. 48.</ref> It is situated on gently sloping terrain surrounded by privately owned forests to the east and west.<ref name="sk7"/> The shrine was established by {{ill|Yamato-hime-no-mikoto|ja|倭姫命|1=Yamatohime-no-mikoto|12=draft}}.<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk"/> The sacred object (*shintai*) of the shrine is a stone.<ref>Kaneko (1983): p. 59.</ref>
=== Shrine Name and location === Although officially named '''Narahara Shrine''' (*奈良波良神社*), it is referred to as '''Narahara Shrine''' (*楢原神社*) in the ''Ritual Records of the Imperial Grand Shrine'' (*皇太神宮儀式帳*).<ref name="sk7"/> The name suggests that the area was once covered with oak (*nara*) trees. The shrine is located in Miyako, which was the site of the "Miyako Misono," a sacred rice field (*shinden*) belonging to {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|12=draft}} (the Outer Shrine of Ise).<ref name="ic"/>
According to the ''Seiyō Gorin Ikkyō'' (*勢陽五鈴遺響*), the name "Miyako" (meaning "capital") derives from an ancient plan to relocate the capital to this area. However, this plan was abandoned due to the emergence of Mt. Daibutsu (*大仏山*), leaving only the name to reflect the intended capital.<ref name="sk7"/> While the ''Report on the Investigation of Shikinaisha'' dismisses this as a "baseless theory," it acknowledges the connection between the shrine's name "'''Nara'''hara" and "Miyako" (都, meaning "capital").<ref name="sk7"/> The Tamaki Town Office explains that the old spelling of Miyako was "Miyako" (*宮子*), meaning "child of the shrine," symbolizing the blessings received from the deities.<ref>{{Cite web|language=ja|url=http://www.tamaki-kanko.jp/html/special_jingu-yukari.html|title=巡る神宮ゆかりの地 (Exploring Places Connected to the Grand Shrine)|publisher=Tamaki Town Office, Industrial Promotion Division|work=Nohon Tamaki Tabi|accessdate=2014-06-01|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20140601033540/http://www.tamaki-kanko.jp/html/special_jingu-yukari.html|archivedate=2014-06-01}}</ref>
== Grounds == The shrine grounds cover an area of 9,500 square meters.<ref name="sk9">Shikinaisha Research Association, 1990, p. 239.</ref> In ancient times, the grounds spanned approximately 5 chō (≈49,585 m²).<ref name="sk9"/> Although the shrine forest was confiscated during the 1871 [[Uchichiryo Edict]], 6,807 m² were returned in 1892.<ref name="sk9"/> The sacred grove is mainly composed of [[hinoki cypress]] trees, with some [[cedar]] and [[pine]] mixed in.<ref name="sk9"/>
Within the grounds, there is a natural stone [[temizuya]] (water basin) donated by devotees.<ref name="ic"/> A stone monument erected by the [[Kishu Domain]] prohibiting the killing of living beings remains on site.<ref name="gk"/>
The shrine pavilion, located near the center of the grounds, is constructed in the [[Shinmei-zukuri]] style with a [[board roof]]. It measures approximately 1.5 meters in length, 1.1 meters in width, and 2.9 meters in height.<ref name="sk0">Shikinaisha Research Association, 1990, p. 240.</ref> It faces south and is enclosed by a single layer of [[tamagaki]] fence with a [[Shinmei torii]] gate.<ref name="sk0"/>
== Enshrined deity == The enshrined deity is **Narahara Hime-no-Mikoto**.<ref name="ic"/> The deity is referred to as "Narawara Hime-no-Mikoto" in the *Ujiyamada City History*.<ref name="uy">Ujiyamada City Office, 1929, p. 11.</ref> She is considered the daughter of **Ōminakami-no-Mikoto**.<ref name="sk7"/>
Worshipped as the [[Ubusunagami]] (guardian deity) of the region,<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk"/> she is classified as a [[Kunitsukami]] (earthly deity).<ref name="tm"/> She is revered as the protector of rice field irrigation, sacred fields, and Narahara.<ref name="tm"/> According to tradition, she dedicated a sacred area to protect [[Yamato-hime-no-mikoto]] during her arduous journey.<ref name="tm"/>
== History == The shrine is believed to have been founded by Yamato-hime-no-mikoto, as mentioned in the *Kōtaijingu Gishiki-chō*.<ref name="sk7"/> Since this document dates back to 804 (Enryaku 23), the shrine's existence predates this period.<ref name="sk7"/> During the [[Medieval period]], the shrine fell into ruin until it was restored in 1663 (Kanbun 3) through the efforts of **Ōnakatomi Seichō (Kawabe Seichō)**.<ref>Shikinaisha Research Association, 1990, pp. 237, 240.</ref>
There is debate regarding the original shrine site, as the description in the *Kōtaijingu Gishiki-chō* does not align with the current location.<ref name="sk7"/> Some theories suggest the original site was deep in Miyako's mountains,<ref>Shikinaisha Research Association, 1990, pp. 237–238.</ref> while others propose it was at **Jōtō Shrine**.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Course 18: Exploring Satoyama Shrines|url=http://www.isekanko.jp/k-cycling/course18.html|publisher=Ise City Tourism Association|accessdate=2014-06-01}}</ref> Despite these theories, the shrine has remained at its current site since its restoration.<ref name="sk7"/>
The current shrine pavilion was rebuilt on October 8, 1980.<ref name="sk9"/>
== Rituals == Although there is no resident [[Kannushi]] (Shinto priest), one **Ikanushi** is appointed to manage daily affairs.<ref name="sk89">Shikinaisha Research Association, 1990, pp. 238–239.</ref>
Key rituals include: - **Kinensai (February 19)**: Prayer for abundant harvest - **Tsukinamisai (June 19 & December 19)**: Monthly festival - **Kannamesai (October 19)**: Offering of the first fruits to the deity - **Niinamesai (November 25)**: Celebration of the new harvest
Additional remote rituals are performed at the **Gojō-den Hall** of the Inner Shrine for New Year’s Day, National Foundation Day (February 11), and other imperial-related festivals.<ref name="sk89"/>
== Surroundings == The shrine is located west of the Miyako settlement, adjacent to the Hirose Seikō Tamaki Factory. It can be accessed via a gravel path after descending a slope.<ref>Ise Culture Publishing, 2008, p. 57.</ref> Nearby are several [[kofun]] (ancient Kofuns).<ref>Mie Prefecture Culture Promotion Division, 2012, p. 8.</ref> * **JR Sangu Line - Taki Station**: Approximately 35-minute walk<ref name="tm"/>. * **Ise Expressway - Tamaki IC**: About 2 minutes (830 m) by car.
== References ==
{{Reflist|2}}
== 参考文献 == * [[伊勢文化舎]] 編『お伊勢さん125社めぐり』別冊『伊勢人』、伊勢文化舎、平成20th year 12月23日、151p. ISBN 978-4-900759-37-4 * 宇治山田市役所 編『宇治山田市史 上巻』宇治山田市役所、昭和4th year 1月20日、862p. * [[学研パブリッシング]]『伊勢神宮に行こう』Gakken Mook神社紀行セレクションvol.1、[[薗田稔]]監修、学研マーケティング、2013rd year 7月4日、82p. ISBN 978-4-05-610047-1 * 金子延夫『玉城町史 第1巻―南伊勢の歴史と伝承―』三重県郷土資料叢書第61集、三重県郷土史料刊行会、昭和58th year 8月10日、276p. * 式内社研究会 編『式内社調査報告 第六巻 東海道1』[[Kogakkan University]]出版部、平成2nd year 2月28日、690p. ISBN 4-87644-080-8 * 三重県環境生活部文化振興課『熊野脇道 三重県 みえ歴史街道ウォーキングマップ』三重県環境生活部文化振興課、2012nd year * 『日本人なら知っておきたい 伊勢神宮と125の社』タツミムック、[[辰巳出版]]、2013rd year 11月1日、127p. ISBN 978-4-7778-1197-7 == See Also == * {{ill|List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|ja|伊勢国の式内社一覧|12=draft}} * {{ill|List of 125 Shrines of Ise|ja|神宮125社の一覧|lt=List of 125 Shrines of Ise|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|3=伊勢国の式内社一覧|12=draft}} * [[下外城田村]]

The shrine pavilion, located near the center of the grounds, is constructed in the [[Shinmei-zukuri]] style with a [[board roof]]. It measures approximately 1.5 meters in length, 1.1 meters in width, and 2.9 meters in height.<ref name="sk0">Shikinaisha Research Association, 1990, p. 240.</ref> It faces south and is enclosed by a single layer of [[tamagaki]] fence with a [[Shinmei torii]] gate.<ref name="sk0"/>
== Enshrined deity == The enshrined deity is **Narahara Hime-no-Mikoto**.<ref name="ic"/> The deity is referred to as "Narawara Hime-no-Mikoto" in the *Ujiyamada City History*.<ref name="uy">Ujiyamada City Office, 1929, p. 11.</ref> She is considered the daughter of **Ōminakami-no-Mikoto**.<ref name="sk7"/>
Worshipped as the [[Ubusunagami]] (guardian deity) of the region,<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk"/> she is classified as a [[Kunitsukami]] (earthly deity).<ref name="tm"/> She is revered as the protector of rice field irrigation, sacred fields, and Narahara.<ref name="tm"/> According to tradition, she dedicated a sacred area to protect [[Yamato-hime-no-mikoto]] during her arduous journey.<ref name="tm"/>
== History == The shrine is believed to have been founded by Yamato-hime-no-mikoto, as mentioned in the *Kōtaijingu Gishiki-chō*.<ref name="sk7"/> Since this document dates back to 804 (Enryaku 23), the shrine's existence predates this period.<ref name="sk7"/> During the [[Medieval period]], the shrine fell into ruin until it was restored in 1663 (Kanbun 3) through the efforts of **Ōnakatomi Seichō (Kawabe Seichō)**.<ref>Shikinaisha Research Association, 1990, pp. 237, 240.</ref>
There is debate regarding the original shrine site, as the description in the *Kōtaijingu Gishiki-chō* does not align with the current location.<ref name="sk7"/> Some theories suggest the original site was deep in Miyako's mountains,<ref>Shikinaisha Research Association, 1990, pp. 237–238.</ref> while others propose it was at **Jōtō Shrine**.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Course 18: Exploring Satoyama Shrines|url=http://www.isekanko.jp/k-cycling/course18.html|publisher=Ise City Tourism Association|accessdate=2014-06-01}}</ref> Despite these theories, the shrine has remained at its current site since its restoration.<ref name="sk7"/>
The current shrine pavilion was rebuilt on October 8, 1980.<ref name="sk9"/>
== Rituals == Although there is no resident [[Kannushi]] (Shinto priest), one **Ikanushi** is appointed to manage daily affairs.<ref name="sk89">Shikinaisha Research Association, 1990, pp. 238–239.</ref>
Key rituals include: - **Kinensai (February 19)**: Prayer for abundant harvest - **Tsukinamisai (June 19 & December 19)**: Monthly festival - **Kannamesai (October 19)**: Offering of the first fruits to the deity - **Niinamesai (November 25)**: Celebration of the new harvest
Additional remote rituals are performed at the **Gojō-den Hall** of the Inner Shrine for New Year’s Day, National Foundation Day (February 11), and other imperial-related festivals.<ref name="sk89"/>
== Surroundings == The shrine is located west of the Miyako settlement, adjacent to the Hirose Seikō Tamaki Factory. It can be accessed via a gravel path after descending a slope.<ref>Ise Culture Publishing, 2008, p. 57.</ref> Nearby are several [[kofun]] (ancient Kofuns).<ref>Mie Prefecture Culture Promotion Division, 2012, p. 8.</ref> * **JR Sangu Line - Taki Station**: Approximately 35-minute walk<ref name="tm"/>. * **Ise Expressway - Tamaki IC**: About 2 minutes (830 m) by car.
== References ==

ISBN 978-4-900759-37-4 * 宇治山田市役所 編『宇治山田市史 上巻』宇治山田市役所、昭和4th year 1月20日、862p. * [[学研パブリッシング]]『伊勢神宮に行こう』Gakken Mook神社紀行セレクションvol.1、[[薗田稔]]監修、学研マーケティング、2013rd year 7月4日、82p. ISBN 978-4-05-610047-1 * 金子延夫『玉城町史 第1巻―南伊勢の歴史と伝承―』三重県郷土資料叢書第61集、三重県郷土史料刊行会、昭和58th year 8月10日、276p. * 式内社研究会 編『式内社調査報告 第六巻 東海道1』[[Kogakkan University]]出版部、平成2nd year 2月28日、690p. ISBN 4-87644-080-8 * 三重県環境生活部文化振興課『熊野脇道 三重県 みえ歴史街道ウォーキングマップ』三重県環境生活部文化振興課、2012nd year * 『日本人なら知っておきたい 伊勢神宮と125の社』タツミムック、[[辰巳出版]]、2013rd year 11月1日、127p. ISBN 978-4-7778-1197-7 == See Also == * {{ill|List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|ja|伊勢国の式内社一覧|qq=draft}} * {{ill|List of 125 Shrines of Ise|ja|神宮125社の一覧|lt=List of 125 Shrines of Ise|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|3=伊勢国の式内社一覧|qq=draft}} * [[下外城田村]]

* [http://www.jingukaikan.jp/125mairi/m08/0803.html 奈良波良神社（ならはらじんじゃ）] - 一般財団法人伊勢神宮崇敬会 * [http://www.town.tamaki.mie.jp/hpdata/kanbun/jinjya.html 神社めぐり] - 玉城町

{{Shrine | Name = {{ill|Narahara Shrine|ja|奈良波良神社|qq=draft}} | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = [[File:Narahara-jinja.jpg|270px]] | location = 833 Yagurado, Miyako, [[Tamaki]], [[Watarai District]], [[Mie Prefecture]], Japan | coordinates = {{coord|34|28|18.5|N|136|37|29.5|E|region:JP|display=inline}} | Map = Japan Mie | deity = {{ill|Narahara-hime-no-mikoto|ja|那良原比女命|qq=draft}} | type = [[Shikinaisha]] (minor Rank), {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|qq=draft}} auxiliary shrine (*sessha*) | Established = Before [[Enryaku era|Enryaku]] 23 (804 CE)<ref name="sk7">Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): p. 237.</ref> | Main Hall = [[Shinmei-zukuri]] | Other Names = | festival = [[Kannamesai]] (Harvest Festival) | Rituals = }}
'''{{ill|Narahara Shrine|ja|奈良波良神社|qq=draft}}''' (*Narahara Jinja*) is an auxiliary shrine (*sessha*) of {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|qq=draft}} (the Inner Shrine of the [[Ise Grand Shrine]]), located in [[Tamaki]], [[Mie Prefecture]], Japan. It Ranks 22nd among the auxiliary shrines of the Inner Shrine.<ref>Ujiyamada City Office (1929): pp. 9–11.</ref>
The name "Narahara" means "oak plain," suggesting that the surrounding area was once a plain abundant with [[Nara tree|Nara]] (oak) trees.<ref name="ic">Ise Bunka Publishing (2008): p. 59.</ref><ref name="gk">Gakken Publishing (2013): p. 67.</ref>
== Overview == The shrine is located at Yagurado 833, Miyako, [[Tamaki]], [[Watarai District]], [[Mie Prefecture]], Japan.<ref name="tm">Tatsumi Publishing (2013): p. 48.</ref> It is situated on gently sloping terrain surrounded by privately owned forests to the east and west.<ref name="sk7"/> The shrine was established by {{ill|Yamato-hime-no-mikoto|ja|倭姫命|1=Yamatohime-no-mikoto|qq=draft}}.<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk"/> The sacred object (*shintai*) of the shrine is a stone.<ref>Kaneko (1983): p. 59.</ref>
=== Shrine Name and location === Although officially named '''Narahara Shrine''' (*奈良波良神社*), it is referred to as '''Narahara Shrine''' (*楢原神社*) in the ''Ritual Records of the Imperial Grand Shrine'' (*皇太神宮儀式帳*).<ref name="sk7"/> The name suggests that the area was once covered with oak (*nara*) trees. The shrine is located in Miyako, which was the site of the "Miyako Misono," a sacred rice field (*shinden*) belonging to {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|qq=draft}} (the Outer Shrine of Ise).<ref name="ic"/>
According to the ''Seiyō Gorin Ikkyō'' (*勢陽五鈴遺響*), the name "Miyako" (meaning "capital") derives from an ancient plan to relocate the capital to this area. However, this plan was abandoned due to the emergence of Mt. Daibutsu (*大仏山*), leaving only the name to reflect the intended capital.<ref name="sk7"/> While the ''Report on the Investigation of Shikinaisha'' dismisses this as a "baseless theory," it acknowledges the connection between the shrine's name "'''Nara'''hara" and "Miyako" (都, meaning "capital").<ref name="sk7"/> The Tamaki Town Office explains that the old spelling of Miyako was "Miyako" (*宮子*), meaning "child of the shrine," symbolizing the blessings received from the deities.<ref>{{Cite web|language=ja|url=http://www.tamaki-kanko.jp/html/special_jingu-yukari.html|title=巡る神宮ゆかりの地 (Exploring Places Connected to the Grand Shrine)|publisher=Tamaki Town Office, Industrial Promotion Division|work=Nohon Tamaki Tabi|accessdate=2014-06-01|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20140601033540/http://www.tamaki-kanko.jp/html/special_jingu-yukari.html|archivedate=2014-06-01}}</ref>
== Grounds == The shrine grounds cover an area of 9,500 square meters.<ref name="sk9">Shikinaisha Research Association, 1990, p. 239.</ref> In ancient times, the grounds spanned approximately 5 chō (≈49,585 m²).<ref name="sk9"/> Although the shrine forest was confiscated during the 1871 [[Uchichiryo Edict]], 6,807 m² were returned in 1892.<ref name="sk9"/> The sacred grove is mainly composed of [[hinoki cypress]] trees, with some [[cedar]] and [[pine]] mixed in.<ref name="sk9"/>
Within the grounds, there is a natural stone [[temizuya]] (water basin) donated by devotees.<ref name="ic"/> A stone monument erected by the [[Kishu Domain]] prohibiting the killing of living beings remains on site.<ref name="gk"/>
The shrine pavilion, located near the center of the grounds, is constructed in the [[Shinmei-zukuri]] style with a [[board roof]]. It measures approximately 1.5 meters in length, 1.1 meters in width, and 2.9 meters in height.<ref name="sk0">Shikinaisha Research Association, 1990, p. 240.</ref> It faces south and is enclosed by a single layer of [[tamagaki]] fence with a [[Shinmei torii]] gate.<ref name="sk0"/>
== Enshrined deity == The enshrined deity is **Narahara Hime-no-Mikoto**.<ref name="ic"/> The deity is referred to as "Narawara Hime-no-Mikoto" in the *Ujiyamada City History*.<ref name="uy">Ujiyamada City Office, 1929, p. 11.</ref> She is considered the daughter of **Ōminakami-no-Mikoto**.<ref name="sk7"/>
Worshipped as the [[Ubusunagami]] (guardian deity) of the region,<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk"/> she is classified as a [[Kunitsukami]] (earthly deity).<ref name="tm"/> She is revered as the protector of rice field irrigation, sacred fields, and Narahara.<ref name="tm"/> According to tradition, she dedicated a sacred area to protect [[Yamato-hime-no-mikoto]] during her arduous journey.<ref name="tm"/>
== History == The shrine is believed to have been founded by Yamato-hime-no-mikoto, as mentioned in the *Kōtaijingu Gishiki-chō*.<ref name="sk7"/> Since this document dates back to 804 (Enryaku 23), the shrine's existence predates this period.<ref name="sk7"/> During the [[Medieval period]], the shrine fell into ruin until it was restored in 1663 (Kanbun 3) through the efforts of **Ōnakatomi Seichō (Kawabe Seichō)**.<ref>Shikinaisha Research Association, 1990, pp. 237, 240.</ref>
There is debate regarding the original shrine site, as the description in the *Kōtaijingu Gishiki-chō* does not align with the current location.<ref name="sk7"/> Some theories suggest the original site was deep in Miyako's mountains,<ref>Shikinaisha Research Association, 1990, pp. 237–238.</ref> while others propose it was at **Jōtō Shrine**.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Course 18: Exploring Satoyama Shrines|url=http://www.isekanko.jp/k-cycling/course18.html|publisher=Ise City Tourism Association|accessdate=2014-06-01}}</ref> Despite these theories, the shrine has remained at its current site since its restoration.<ref name="sk7"/>
The current shrine pavilion was rebuilt on October 8, 1980.<ref name="sk9"/>
== Rituals == Although there is no resident [[Kannushi]] (Shinto priest), one **Ikanushi** is appointed to manage daily affairs.<ref name="sk89">Shikinaisha Research Association, 1990, pp. 238–239.</ref>
Key rituals include: - **Kinensai (February 19)**: Prayer for abundant harvest - **Tsukinamisai (June 19 & December 19)**: Monthly festival - **Kannamesai (October 19)**: Offering of the first fruits to the deity - **Niinamesai (November 25)**: Celebration of the new harvest
Additional remote rituals are performed at the **Gojō-den Hall** of the Inner Shrine for New Year’s Day, National Foundation Day (February 11), and other imperial-related festivals.<ref name="sk89"/>
== Surroundings == The shrine is located west of the Miyako settlement, adjacent to the Hirose Seikō Tamaki Factory. It can be accessed via a gravel path after descending a slope.<ref>Ise Culture Publishing, 2008, p. 57.</ref> Nearby are several [[kofun]] (ancient Kofuns).<ref>Mie Prefecture Culture Promotion Division, 2012, p. 8.</ref> * **JR Sangu Line - Taki Station**: Approximately 35-minute walk<ref name="tm"/>. * **Ise Expressway - Tamaki IC**: About 2 minutes (830 m) by car.
== References ==
{{Reflist|2}}
== 参考文献 == * [[伊勢文化舎]] 編『お伊勢さん125社めぐり』別冊『伊勢人』、伊勢文化舎、平成20th year 12月23日、151p. ISBN 978-4-900759-37-4 * 宇治山田市役所 編『宇治山田市史 上巻』宇治山田市役所、昭和4th year 1月20日、862p. * [[学研パブリッシング]]『伊勢神宮に行こう』Gakken Mook神社紀行セレクションvol.1、[[薗田稔]]監修、学研マーケティング、2013rd year 7月4日、82p. ISBN 978-4-05-610047-1 * 金子延夫『玉城町史 第1巻―南伊勢の歴史と伝承―』三重県郷土資料叢書第61集、三重県郷土史料刊行会、昭和58th year 8月10日、276p. * 式内社研究会 編『式内社調査報告 第六巻 東海道1』[[Kogakkan University]]出版部、平成2nd year 2月28日、690p. ISBN 4-87644-080-8 * 三重県環境生活部文化振興課『熊野脇道 三重県 みえ歴史街道ウォーキングマップ』三重県環境生活部文化振興課、2012nd year * 『日本人なら知っておきたい 伊勢神宮と125の社』タツミムック、[[辰巳出版]]、2013rd year 11月1日、127p. ISBN 978-4-7778-1197-7 == See Also == * {{ill|List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|ja|伊勢国の式内社一覧|qq=draft}} * {{ill|List of 125 Shrines of Ise|ja|神宮125社の一覧|lt=List of 125 Shrines of Ise|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|3=伊勢国の式内社一覧|qq=draft}} * [[下外城田村]]
== External Links == {{ external media | align = | width = 230px | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image1 = [http://www.sengukan.jp/wp-content/themes/sengukan/images/shrine/area04/area04_04.jpg 奈良波良神社] }}
* [http://www.jingukaikan.jp/125mairi/m08/0803.html 奈良波良神社（ならはらじんじゃ）] - 一般財団法人伊勢神宮崇敬会 * [http://www.town.tamaki.mie.jp/hpdata/kanbun/jinjya.html 神社めぐり] - 玉城町

There is debate regarding the original shrine site, as the description in the *Kōtaijingu Gishiki-chō* does not align with the current location.<ref name="sk7"/> Some theories suggest the original site was deep in Miyako's mountains,<ref>Shikinaisha Research Association, 1990, pp. 237–238.</ref> while others propose it was at **Jōtō Shrine**.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Course 18: Exploring Satoyama Shrines|url=http://www.isekanko.jp/k-cycling/course18.html|publisher=Ise City Tourism Association|accessdate=2014-06-01}}</ref> Despite these theories, the shrine has remained at its current site since its restoration.<ref name="sk7"/>
The current shrine pavilion was rebuilt on October 8, 1980.<ref name="sk9"/>
== Rituals == Although there is no resident [[Kannushi]] (Shinto priest), one **Ikanushi** is appointed to manage daily affairs.<ref name="sk89">Shikinaisha Research Association, 1990, pp. 238–239.</ref>
Key rituals include: - **Kinensai (February 19)**: Prayer for abundant harvest - **Tsukinamisai (June 19 & December 19)**: Monthly festival - **Kannamesai (October 19)**: Offering of the first fruits to the deity - **Niinamesai (November 25)**: Celebration of the new harvest
Additional remote rituals are performed at the **Gojō-den Hall** of the Inner Shrine for New Year’s Day, National Foundation Day (February 11), and other imperial-related festivals.<ref name="sk89"/>
== Surroundings == The shrine is located west of the Miyako settlement, adjacent to the Hirose Seikō Tamaki Factory. It can be accessed via a gravel path after descending a slope.<ref>Ise Culture Publishing, 2008, p. 57.</ref> Nearby are several [[kofun]] (ancient Kofuns).<ref>Mie Prefecture Culture Promotion Division, 2012, p. 8.</ref> * **JR Sangu Line - Taki Station**: Approximately 35-minute walk<ref name="tm"/>. * **Ise Expressway - Tamaki IC**: About 2 minutes (830 m) by car.

revision.comment: Moving from [[Category:玉城町の歴史]] to [[Category:Tamaki,_Mie]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{Shrine | Name = {{ill|Narahara Shrine|ja|奈良波良神社|qq=draft}} | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = [[File:Narahara-jinja.jpg|270px]] | location = 833 Yagurado, Miyako, [[Tamaki]], [[Watarai District]], [[Mie Prefecture]], Japan | coordinates = {{coord|34|28|18.5|N|136|37|29.5|E|region:JP|display=inline}} | Map = Japan Mie | deity = {{ill|Narahara-hime-no-mikoto|ja|那良原比女命|qq=draft}} | type = [[Shikinaisha]] (minor Rank), {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|qq=draft}} auxiliary shrine (*sessha*) | Established = Before [[Enryaku era|Enryaku]] 23 (804 CE)<ref name="sk7">Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): p. 237.</ref> | Main Hall = [[Shinmei-zukuri]] | Other Names = | festival = [[Kannamesai]] (Harvest Festival) | Rituals = }}
'''{{ill|Narahara Shrine|ja|奈良波良神社|qq=draft}}''' (*Narahara Jinja*) is an auxiliary shrine (*sessha*) of {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|qq=draft}} (the Inner Shrine of the [[Ise Grand Shrine]]), located in [[Tamaki]], [[Mie Prefecture]], Japan. It Ranks 22nd among the auxiliary shrines of the Inner Shrine.<ref>Ujiyamada City Office (1929): pp. 9–11.</ref>
The name "Narahara" means "oak plain," suggesting that the surrounding area was once a plain abundant with [[Nara tree|Nara]] (oak) trees.<ref name="ic">Ise Bunka Publishing (2008): p. 59.</ref><ref name="gk">Gakken Publishing (2013): p. 67.</ref>
== Overview == The shrine is located at Yagurado 833, Miyako, [[Tamaki]], [[Watarai District]], [[Mie Prefecture]], Japan.<ref name="tm">Tatsumi Publishing (2013): p. 48.</ref> It is situated on gently sloping terrain surrounded by privately owned forests to the east and west.<ref name="sk7"/> The shrine was established by {{ill|Yamato-hime-no-mikoto|ja|倭姫命|1=Yamatohime-no-mikoto|qq=draft}}.<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk"/> The sacred object (*shintai*) of the shrine is a stone.<ref>Kaneko (1983): p. 59.</ref>
=== Shrine Name and location === Although officially named '''Narahara Shrine''' (*奈良波良神社*), it is referred to as '''Narahara Shrine''' (*楢原神社*) in the ''Ritual Records of the Imperial Grand Shrine'' (*皇太神宮儀式帳*).<ref name="sk7"/> The name suggests that the area was once covered with oak (*nara*) trees. The shrine is located in Miyako, which was the site of the "Miyako Misono," a sacred rice field (*shinden*) belonging to {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|qq=draft}} (the Outer Shrine of Ise).<ref name="ic"/>
According to the ''Seiyō Gorin Ikkyō'' (*勢陽五鈴遺響*), the name "Miyako" (meaning "capital") derives from an ancient plan to relocate the capital to this area. However, this plan was abandoned due to the emergence of Mt. Daibutsu (*大仏山*), leaving only the name to reflect the intended capital.<ref name="sk7"/> While the ''Report on the Investigation of Shikinaisha'' dismisses this as a "baseless theory," it acknowledges the connection between the shrine's name "'''Nara'''hara" and "Miyako" (都, meaning "capital").<ref name="sk7"/> The Tamaki Town Office explains that the old spelling of Miyako was "Miyako" (*宮子*), meaning "child of the shrine," symbolizing the blessings received from the deities.<ref>{{Cite web|language=ja|url=http://www.tamaki-kanko.jp/html/special_jingu-yukari.html|title=巡る神宮ゆかりの地 (Exploring Places Connected to the Grand Shrine)|publisher=Tamaki Town Office, Industrial Promotion Division|work=Nohon Tamaki Tabi|accessdate=2014-06-01|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20140601033540/http://www.tamaki-kanko.jp/html/special_jingu-yukari.html|archivedate=2014-06-01}}</ref>
== Grounds == The shrine grounds cover an area of 9,500 square meters.<ref name="sk9">Shikinaisha Research Association, 1990, p. 239.</ref> In ancient times, the grounds spanned approximately 5 chō (≈49,585 m²).<ref name="sk9"/> Although the shrine forest was confiscated during the 1871 [[Uchichiryo Edict]], 6,807 m² were returned in 1892.<ref name="sk9"/> The sacred grove is mainly composed of [[hinoki cypress]] trees, with some [[cedar]] and [[pine]] mixed in.<ref name="sk9"/>
Within the grounds, there is a natural stone [[temizuya]] (water basin) donated by devotees.<ref name="ic"/> A stone monument erected by the [[Kishu Domain]] prohibiting the killing of living beings remains on site.<ref name="gk"/>
The shrine pavilion, located near the center of the grounds, is constructed in the [[Shinmei-zukuri]] style with a [[board roof]]. It measures approximately 1.5 meters in length, 1.1 meters in width, and 2.9 meters in height.<ref name="sk0">Shikinaisha Research Association, 1990, p. 240.</ref> It faces south and is enclosed by a single layer of [[tamagaki]] fence with a [[Shinmei torii]] gate.<ref name="sk0"/>
== Enshrined deity == The enshrined deity is **Narahara Hime-no-Mikoto**.<ref name="ic"/> The deity is referred to as "Narawara Hime-no-Mikoto" in the *Ujiyamada City History*.<ref name="uy">Ujiyamada City Office, 1929, p. 11.</ref> She is considered the daughter of **Ōminakami-no-Mikoto**.<ref name="sk7"/>
Worshipped as the [[Ubusunagami]] (guardian deity) of the region,<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk"/> she is classified as a [[Kunitsukami]] (earthly deity).<ref name="tm"/> She is revered as the protector of rice field irrigation, sacred fields, and Narahara.<ref name="tm"/> According to tradition, she dedicated a sacred area to protect [[Yamato-hime-no-mikoto]] during her arduous journey.<ref name="tm"/>
== History == The shrine is believed to have been founded by Yamato-hime-no-mikoto, as mentioned in the *Kōtaijingu Gishiki-chō*.<ref name="sk7"/> Since this document dates back to 804 (Enryaku 23), the shrine's existence predates this period.<ref name="sk7"/> During the [[Medieval period]], the shrine fell into ruin until it was restored in 1663 (Kanbun 3) through the efforts of **Ōnakatomi Seichō (Kawabe Seichō)**.<ref>Shikinaisha Research Association, 1990, pp. 237, 240.</ref>
There is debate regarding the original shrine site, as the description in the *Kōtaijingu Gishiki-chō* does not align with the current location.<ref name="sk7"/> Some theories suggest the original site was deep in Miyako's mountains,<ref>Shikinaisha Research Association, 1990, pp. 237–238.</ref> while others propose it was at **Jōtō Shrine**.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Course 18: Exploring Satoyama Shrines|url=http://www.isekanko.jp/k-cycling/course18.html|publisher=Ise City Tourism Association|accessdate=2014-06-01}}</ref> Despite these theories, the shrine has remained at its current site since its restoration.<ref name="sk7"/>
The current shrine pavilion was rebuilt on October 8, 1980.<ref name="sk9"/>
== Rituals == Although there is no resident [[Kannushi]] (Shinto priest), one **Ikanushi** is appointed to manage daily affairs.<ref name="sk89">Shikinaisha Research Association, 1990, pp. 238–239.</ref>
Key rituals include: - **Kinensai (February 19)**: Prayer for abundant harvest - **Tsukinamisai (June 19 & December 19)**: Monthly festival - **Kannamesai (October 19)**: Offering of the first fruits to the deity - **Niinamesai (November 25)**: Celebration of the new harvest
Additional remote rituals are performed at the **Gojō-den Hall** of the Inner Shrine for New Year’s Day, National Foundation Day (February 11), and other imperial-related festivals.<ref name="sk89"/>
== Surroundings == The shrine is located west of the Miyako settlement, adjacent to the Hirose Seikō Tamaki Factory. It can be accessed via a gravel path after descending a slope.<ref>Ise Culture Publishing, 2008, p. 57.</ref> Nearby are several [[kofun]] (ancient Kofuns).<ref>Mie Prefecture Culture Promotion Division, 2012, p. 8.</ref> * **JR Sangu Line - Taki Station**: Approximately 35-minute walk<ref name="tm"/>. * **Ise Expressway - Tamaki IC**: About 2 minutes (830 m) by car.
== References ==

== 参考文献 == * [[伊勢文化舎]] 編『お伊勢さん125社めぐり』別冊『伊勢人』、伊勢文化舎、平成20th year 12月23日、151p. ISBN 978-4-900759-37-4 * 宇治山田市役所 編『宇治山田市史 上巻』宇治山田市役所、昭和4th year 1月20日、862p. * [[学研パブリッシング]]『伊勢神宮に行こう』Gakken Mook神社紀行セレクションvol.1、[[薗田稔]]監修、学研マーケティング、2013rd year 7月4日、82p. ISBN 978-4-05-610047-1 * 金子延夫『玉城町史 第1巻―南伊勢の歴史と伝承―』三重県郷土資料叢書第61集、三重県郷土史料刊行会、昭和58th year 8月10日、276p. * 式内社研究会 編『式内社調査報告 第六巻 東海道1』[[Kogakkan University]]出版部、平成2nd year 2月28日、690p. ISBN 4-87644-080-8 * 三重県環境生活部文化振興課『熊野脇道 三重県 みえ歴史街道ウォーキングマップ』三重県環境生活部文化振興課、2012nd year * 『日本人なら知っておきたい 伊勢神宮と125の社』タツミムック、[[辰巳出版]]、2013rd year 11月1日、127p. ISBN 978-4-7778-1197-7 == See Also == * {{ill|List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|ja|伊勢国の式内社一覧|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|qq=draft}} * {{ill|List of 125 Shrines of Ise|ja|神宮125社の一覧|lt=List of 125 Shrines of Ise|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|3=伊勢国の式内社一覧|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|qq=draft}} * [[下外城田村]]
== External Links == {{ external media | align = | width = 230px | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image1 = [http://www.sengukan.jp/wp-content/themes/sengukan/images/shrine/area04/area04_04.jpg 奈良波良神社] }}
* [http://www.jingukaikan.jp/125mairi/m08/0803.html 奈良波良神社（ならはらじんじゃ）] - 一般財団法人伊勢神宮崇敬会 * [http://www.town.tamaki.mie.jp/hpdata/kanbun/jinjya.html 神社めぐり] - 玉城町

revision.comment: Removing from [[Category:玉城町の歴史]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{Shrine | Name = {{ill|Narahara Shrine|ja|奈良波良神社|qq=draft}} | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = [[File:Narahara-jinja.jpg|270px]] | location = 833 Yagurado, Miyako, [[Tamaki]], [[Watarai District]], [[Mie Prefecture]], Japan | coordinates = {{coord|34|28|18.5|N|136|37|29.5|E|region:JP|display=inline}} | Map = Japan Mie | deity = {{ill|Narahara-hime-no-mikoto|ja|那良原比女命|qq=draft}} | type = [[Shikinaisha]] (minor Rank), {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|qq=draft}} auxiliary shrine (*sessha*) | Established = Before [[Enryaku era|Enryaku]] 23 (804 CE)<ref name="sk7">Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): p. 237.</ref> | Main Hall = [[Shinmei-zukuri]] | Other Names = | festival = [[Kannamesai]] (Harvest Festival) | Rituals = }}
'''{{ill|Narahara Shrine|ja|奈良波良神社|qq=draft}}''' (*Narahara Jinja*) is an auxiliary shrine (*sessha*) of {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|qq=draft}} (the Inner Shrine of the [[Ise Grand Shrine]]), located in [[Tamaki]], [[Mie Prefecture]], Japan. It Ranks 22nd among the auxiliary shrines of the Inner Shrine.<ref>Ujiyamada City Office (1929): pp. 9–11.</ref>
The name "Narahara" means "oak plain," suggesting that the surrounding area was once a plain abundant with [[Nara tree|Nara]] (oak) trees.<ref name="ic">Ise Bunka Publishing (2008): p. 59.</ref><ref name="gk">Gakken Publishing (2013): p. 67.</ref>
== Overview == The shrine is located at Yagurado 833, Miyako, [[Tamaki]], [[Watarai District]], [[Mie Prefecture]], Japan.<ref name="tm">Tatsumi Publishing (2013): p. 48.</ref> It is situated on gently sloping terrain surrounded by privately owned forests to the east and west.<ref name="sk7"/> The shrine was established by {{ill|Yamato-hime-no-mikoto|ja|倭姫命|1=Yamatohime-no-mikoto|qq=draft}}.<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk"/> The sacred object (*shintai*) of the shrine is a stone.<ref>Kaneko (1983): p. 59.</ref>
=== Shrine Name and location === Although officially named '''Narahara Shrine''' (*奈良波良神社*), it is referred to as '''Narahara Shrine''' (*楢原神社*) in the ''Ritual Records of the Imperial Grand Shrine'' (*皇太神宮儀式帳*).<ref name="sk7"/> The name suggests that the area was once covered with oak (*nara*) trees. The shrine is located in Miyako, which was the site of the "Miyako Misono," a sacred rice field (*shinden*) belonging to {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|qq=draft}} (the Outer Shrine of Ise).<ref name="ic"/>
According to the ''Seiyō Gorin Ikkyō'' (*勢陽五鈴遺響*), the name "Miyako" (meaning "capital") derives from an ancient plan to relocate the capital to this area. However, this plan was abandoned due to the emergence of Mt. Daibutsu (*大仏山*), leaving only the name to reflect the intended capital.<ref name="sk7"/> While the ''Report on the Investigation of Shikinaisha'' dismisses this as a "baseless theory," it acknowledges the connection between the shrine's name "'''Nara'''hara" and "Miyako" (都, meaning "capital").<ref name="sk7"/> The Tamaki Town Office explains that the old spelling of Miyako was "Miyako" (*宮子*), meaning "child of the shrine," symbolizing the blessings received from the deities.<ref>{{Cite web|language=ja|url=http://www.tamaki-kanko.jp/html/special_jingu-yukari.html|title=巡る神宮ゆかりの地 (Exploring Places Connected to the Grand Shrine)|publisher=Tamaki Town Office, Industrial Promotion Division|work=Nohon Tamaki Tabi|accessdate=2014-06-01|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20140601033540/http://www.tamaki-kanko.jp/html/special_jingu-yukari.html|archivedate=2014-06-01}}</ref>
== Grounds == The shrine grounds cover an area of 9,500 square meters.<ref name="sk9">Shikinaisha Research Association, 1990, p. 239.</ref> In ancient times, the grounds spanned approximately 5 chō (≈49,585 m²).<ref name="sk9"/> Although the shrine forest was confiscated during the 1871 [[Uchichiryo Edict]], 6,807 m² were returned in 1892.<ref name="sk9"/> The sacred grove is mainly composed of [[hinoki cypress]] trees, with some [[cedar]] and [[pine]] mixed in.<ref name="sk9"/>
Within the grounds, there is a natural stone [[temizuya]] (water basin) donated by devotees.<ref name="ic"/> A stone monument erected by the [[Kishu Domain]] prohibiting the killing of living beings remains on site.<ref name="gk"/>
The shrine pavilion, located near the center of the grounds, is constructed in the [[Shinmei-zukuri]] style with a [[board roof]]. It measures approximately 1.5 meters in length, 1.1 meters in width, and 2.9 meters in height.<ref name="sk0">Shikinaisha Research Association, 1990, p. 240.</ref> It faces south and is enclosed by a single layer of [[tamagaki]] fence with a [[Shinmei torii]] gate.<ref name="sk0"/>
== Enshrined deity == The enshrined deity is **Narahara Hime-no-Mikoto**.<ref name="ic"/> The deity is referred to as "Narawara Hime-no-Mikoto" in the *Ujiyamada City History*.<ref name="uy">Ujiyamada City Office, 1929, p. 11.</ref> She is considered the daughter of **Ōminakami-no-Mikoto**.<ref name="sk7"/>
Worshipped as the [[Ubusunagami]] (guardian deity) of the region,<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk"/> she is classified as a [[Kunitsukami]] (earthly deity).<ref name="tm"/> She is revered as the protector of rice field irrigation, sacred fields, and Narahara.<ref name="tm"/> According to tradition, she dedicated a sacred area to protect [[Yamato-hime-no-mikoto]] during her arduous journey.<ref name="tm"/>
== History == The shrine is believed to have been founded by Yamato-hime-no-mikoto, as mentioned in the *Kōtaijingu Gishiki-chō*.<ref name="sk7"/> Since this document dates back to 804 (Enryaku 23), the shrine's existence predates this period.<ref name="sk7"/> During the [[Medieval period]], the shrine fell into ruin until it was restored in 1663 (Kanbun 3) through the efforts of **Ōnakatomi Seichō (Kawabe Seichō)**.<ref>Shikinaisha Research Association, 1990, pp. 237, 240.</ref>
There is debate regarding the original shrine site, as the description in the *Kōtaijingu Gishiki-chō* does not align with the current location.<ref name="sk7"/> Some theories suggest the original site was deep in Miyako's mountains,<ref>Shikinaisha Research Association, 1990, pp. 237–238.</ref> while others propose it was at **Jōtō Shrine**.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Course 18: Exploring Satoyama Shrines|url=http://www.isekanko.jp/k-cycling/course18.html|publisher=Ise City Tourism Association|accessdate=2014-06-01}}</ref> Despite these theories, the shrine has remained at its current site since its restoration.<ref name="sk7"/>
The current shrine pavilion was rebuilt on October 8, 1980.<ref name="sk9"/>
== Rituals == Although there is no resident [[Kannushi]] (Shinto priest), one **Ikanushi** is appointed to manage daily affairs.<ref name="sk89">Shikinaisha Research Association, 1990, pp. 238–239.</ref>
Key rituals include: - **Kinensai (February 19)**: Prayer for abundant harvest - **Tsukinamisai (June 19 & December 19)**: Monthly festival - **Kannamesai (October 19)**: Offering of the first fruits to the deity - **Niinamesai (November 25)**: Celebration of the new harvest
Additional remote rituals are performed at the **Gojō-den Hall** of the Inner Shrine for New Year’s Day, National Foundation Day (February 11), and other imperial-related festivals.<ref name="sk89"/>
== Surroundings == The shrine is located west of the Miyako settlement, adjacent to the Hirose Seikō Tamaki Factory. It can be accessed via a gravel path after descending a slope.<ref>Ise Culture Publishing, 2008, p. 57.</ref> Nearby are several [[kofun]] (ancient Kofuns).<ref>Mie Prefecture Culture Promotion Division, 2012, p. 8.</ref> * **JR Sangu Line - Taki Station**: Approximately 35-minute walk<ref name="tm"/>. * **Ise Expressway - Tamaki IC**: About 2 minutes (830 m) by car.
== References ==
{{Reflist|2}}
== 参考文献 == * [[伊勢文化舎]] 編『お伊勢さん125社めぐり』別冊『伊勢人』、伊勢文化舎、平成20th year 12月23日、151p. ISBN 978-4-900759-37-4 * 宇治山田市役所 編『宇治山田市史 上巻』宇治山田市役所、昭和4th year 1月20日、862p. * [[学研パブリッシング]]『伊勢神宮に行こう』Gakken Mook神社紀行セレクションvol.1、[[薗田稔]]監修、学研マーケティング、2013rd year 7月4日、82p. ISBN 978-4-05-610047-1 * 金子延夫『玉城町史 第1巻―南伊勢の歴史と伝承―』三重県郷土資料叢書第61集、三重県郷土史料刊行会、昭和58th year 8月10日、276p. * 式内社研究会 編『式内社調査報告 第六巻 東海道1』[[Kogakkan University]]出版部、平成2nd year 2月28日、690p. ISBN 4-87644-080-8 * 三重県環境生活部文化振興課『熊野脇道 三重県 みえ歴史街道ウォーキングマップ』三重県環境生活部文化振興課、2012nd year * 『日本人なら知っておきたい 伊勢神宮と125の社』タツミムック、[[辰巳出版]]、2013rd year 11月1日、127p. ISBN 978-4-7778-1197-7 == See Also == * {{ill|List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|ja|伊勢国の式内社一覧|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|qq=draft}} * {{ill|List of 125 Shrines of Ise|ja|神宮125社の一覧|lt=List of 125 Shrines of Ise|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|3=伊勢国の式内社一覧|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|qq=draft}} * [[下外城田村]]

revision.comment: Bot: remove [[Category:奈良波良神社]] from page
revision.text:
{{Shrine | Name = {{ill|Narahara Shrine|ja|奈良波良神社|qq=draft}} | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = [[File:Narahara-jinja.jpg|270px]] | location = 833 Yagurado, Miyako, [[Tamaki]], [[Watarai District]], [[Mie Prefecture]], Japan | coordinates = {{coord|34|28|18.5|N|136|37|29.5|E|region:JP|display=inline}} | Map = Japan Mie | deity = {{ill|Narahara-hime-no-mikoto|ja|那良原比女命|qq=draft}} | type = [[Shikinaisha]] (minor Rank), {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|qq=draft}} auxiliary shrine (*sessha*) | Established = Before [[Enryaku era|Enryaku]] 23 (804 CE)<ref name="sk7">Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): p. 237.</ref> | Main Hall = [[Shinmei-zukuri]] | Other Names = | festival = [[Kannamesai]] (Harvest Festival) | Rituals = }}
'''{{ill|Narahara Shrine|ja|奈良波良神社|qq=draft}}''' (*Narahara Jinja*) is an auxiliary shrine (*sessha*) of {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|qq=draft}} (the Inner Shrine of the [[Ise Grand Shrine]]), located in [[Tamaki]], [[Mie Prefecture]], Japan. It Ranks 22nd among the auxiliary shrines of the Inner Shrine.<ref>Ujiyamada City Office (1929): pp. 9–11.</ref>
The name "Narahara" means "oak plain," suggesting that the surrounding area was once a plain abundant with [[Nara tree|Nara]] (oak) trees.<ref name="ic">Ise Bunka Publishing (2008): p. 59.</ref><ref name="gk">Gakken Publishing (2013): p. 67.</ref>
== Overview == The shrine is located at Yagurado 833, Miyako, [[Tamaki]], [[Watarai District]], [[Mie Prefecture]], Japan.<ref name="tm">Tatsumi Publishing (2013): p. 48.</ref> It is situated on gently sloping terrain surrounded by privately owned forests to the east and west.<ref name="sk7"/> The shrine was established by {{ill|Yamato-hime-no-mikoto|ja|倭姫命|1=Yamatohime-no-mikoto|qq=draft}}.<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk"/> The sacred object (*shintai*) of the shrine is a stone.<ref>Kaneko (1983): p. 59.</ref>
=== Shrine Name and location === Although officially named '''Narahara Shrine''' (*奈良波良神社*), it is referred to as '''Narahara Shrine''' (*楢原神社*) in the ''Ritual Records of the Imperial Grand Shrine'' (*皇太神宮儀式帳*).<ref name="sk7"/> The name suggests that the area was once covered with oak (*nara*) trees. The shrine is located in Miyako, which was the site of the "Miyako Misono," a sacred rice field (*shinden*) belonging to {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|qq=draft}} (the Outer Shrine of Ise).<ref name="ic"/>
According to the ''Seiyō Gorin Ikkyō'' (*勢陽五鈴遺響*), the name "Miyako" (meaning "capital") derives from an ancient plan to relocate the capital to this area. However, this plan was abandoned due to the emergence of Mt. Daibutsu (*大仏山*), leaving only the name to reflect the intended capital.<ref name="sk7"/> While the ''Report on the Investigation of Shikinaisha'' dismisses this as a "baseless theory," it acknowledges the connection between the shrine's name "'''Nara'''hara" and "Miyako" (都, meaning "capital").<ref name="sk7"/> The Tamaki Town Office explains that the old spelling of Miyako was "Miyako" (*宮子*), meaning "child of the shrine," symbolizing the blessings received from the deities.<ref>{{Cite web|language=ja|url=http://www.tamaki-kanko.jp/html/special_jingu-yukari.html|title=巡る神宮ゆかりの地 (Exploring Places Connected to the Grand Shrine)|publisher=Tamaki Town Office, Industrial Promotion Division|work=Nohon Tamaki Tabi|accessdate=2014-06-01|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20140601033540/http://www.tamaki-kanko.jp/html/special_jingu-yukari.html|archivedate=2014-06-01}}</ref>
== Grounds == The shrine grounds cover an area of 9,500 square meters.<ref name="sk9">Shikinaisha Research Association, 1990, p. 239.</ref> In ancient times, the grounds spanned approximately 5 chō (≈49,585 m²).<ref name="sk9"/> Although the shrine forest was confiscated during the 1871 [[Uchichiryo Edict]], 6,807 m² were returned in 1892.<ref name="sk9"/> The sacred grove is mainly composed of [[hinoki cypress]] trees, with some [[cedar]] and [[pine]] mixed in.<ref name="sk9"/>
Within the grounds, there is a natural stone [[temizuya]] (water basin) donated by devotees.<ref name="ic"/> A stone monument erected by the [[Kishu Domain]] prohibiting the killing of living beings remains on site.<ref name="gk"/>
The shrine pavilion, located near the center of the grounds, is constructed in the [[Shinmei-zukuri]] style with a [[board roof]]. It measures approximately 1.5 meters in length, 1.1 meters in width, and 2.9 meters in height.<ref name="sk0">Shikinaisha Research Association, 1990, p. 240.</ref> It faces south and is enclosed by a single layer of [[tamagaki]] fence with a [[Shinmei torii]] gate.<ref name="sk0"/>
== Enshrined deity == The enshrined deity is **Narahara Hime-no-Mikoto**.<ref name="ic"/> The deity is referred to as "Narawara Hime-no-Mikoto" in the *Ujiyamada City History*.<ref name="uy">Ujiyamada City Office, 1929, p. 11.</ref> She is considered the daughter of **Ōminakami-no-Mikoto**.<ref name="sk7"/>
Worshipped as the [[Ubusunagami]] (guardian deity) of the region,<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk"/> she is classified as a [[Kunitsukami]] (earthly deity).<ref name="tm"/> She is revered as the protector of rice field irrigation, sacred fields, and Narahara.<ref name="tm"/> According to tradition, she dedicated a sacred area to protect [[Yamato-hime-no-mikoto]] during her arduous journey.<ref name="tm"/>
== History == The shrine is believed to have been founded by Yamato-hime-no-mikoto, as mentioned in the *Kōtaijingu Gishiki-chō*.<ref name="sk7"/> Since this document dates back to 804 (Enryaku 23), the shrine's existence predates this period.<ref name="sk7"/> During the [[Medieval period]], the shrine fell into ruin until it was restored in 1663 (Kanbun 3) through the efforts of **Ōnakatomi Seichō (Kawabe Seichō)**.<ref>Shikinaisha Research Association, 1990, pp. 237, 240.</ref>
There is debate regarding the original shrine site, as the description in the *Kōtaijingu Gishiki-chō* does not align with the current location.<ref name="sk7"/> Some theories suggest the original site was deep in Miyako's mountains,<ref>Shikinaisha Research Association, 1990, pp. 237–238.</ref> while others propose it was at **Jōtō Shrine**.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Course 18: Exploring Satoyama Shrines|url=http://www.isekanko.jp/k-cycling/course18.html|publisher=Ise City Tourism Association|accessdate=2014-06-01}}</ref> Despite these theories, the shrine has remained at its current site since its restoration.<ref name="sk7"/>
The current shrine pavilion was rebuilt on October 8, 1980.<ref name="sk9"/>
== Rituals == Although there is no resident [[Kannushi]] (Shinto priest), one **Ikanushi** is appointed to manage daily affairs.<ref name="sk89">Shikinaisha Research Association, 1990, pp. 238–239.</ref>
Key rituals include: - **Kinensai (February 19)**: Prayer for abundant harvest - **Tsukinamisai (June 19 & December 19)**: Monthly festival - **Kannamesai (October 19)**: Offering of the first fruits to the deity - **Niinamesai (November 25)**: Celebration of the new harvest
Additional remote rituals are performed at the **Gojō-den Hall** of the Inner Shrine for New Year’s Day, National Foundation Day (February 11), and other imperial-related festivals.<ref name="sk89"/>
== Surroundings == The shrine is located west of the Miyako settlement, adjacent to the Hirose Seikō Tamaki Factory. It can be accessed via a gravel path after descending a slope.<ref>Ise Culture Publishing, 2008, p. 57.</ref> Nearby are several [[kofun]] (ancient Kofuns).<ref>Mie Prefecture Culture Promotion Division, 2012, p. 8.</ref> * **JR Sangu Line - Taki Station**: Approximately 35-minute walk<ref name="tm"/>. * **Ise Expressway - Tamaki IC**: About 2 minutes (830 m) by car.
== References ==
{{Reflist|2}}

ISBN 978-4-900759-37-4 * 宇治山田市役所 編『宇治山田市史 上巻』宇治山田市役所、昭和4th year 1月20日、862p. * [[学研パブリッシング]]『伊勢神宮に行こう』Gakken Mook神社紀行セレクションvol.1、[[薗田稔]]監修、学研マーケティング、2013rd year 7月4日、82p. ISBN 978-4-05-610047-1 * 金子延夫『玉城町史 第1巻―南伊勢の歴史と伝承―』三重県郷土資料叢書第61集、三重県郷土史料刊行会、昭和58th year 8月10日、276p. * 式内社研究会 編『式内社調査報告 第六巻 東海道1』[[Kogakkan University]]出版部、平成2nd year 2月28日、690p. ISBN 4-87644-080-8 * 三重県環境生活部文化振興課『熊野脇道 三重県 みえ歴史街道ウォーキングマップ』三重県環境生活部文化振興課、2012nd year * 『日本人なら知っておきたい 伊勢神宮と125の社』タツミムック、[[辰巳出版]]、2013rd year 11月1日、127p. ISBN 978-4-7778-1197-7 == See Also == * {{ill|List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|ja|伊勢国の式内社一覧|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|qq=draft}} * {{ill|List of 125 Shrines of Ise|ja|神宮125社の一覧|lt=List of 125 Shrines of Ise|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|3=伊勢国の式内社一覧|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|qq=draft}} * [[下外城田村]]

revision.comment: Bot: fix redirect [[Kannamesai]] → [[Kannamesai Festival]]
revision.text:
{{Shrine | Name = {{ill|Narahara Shrine|ja|奈良波良神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Narahara Shrine|qq=}} | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = [[File:Narahara-jinja.jpg|270px]] | location = 833 Yagurado, Miyako, [[Tamaki]], [[Watarai District]], [[Mie Prefecture]], Japan | coordinates = {{coord|34|28|18.5|N|136|37|29.5|E|region:JP|display=inline}} | Map = Japan Mie | deity = {{ill|Narahara-hime-no-mikoto|ja|那良原比女命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Narahara-hime-no-mikoto|qq=}} | type = [[Shikinaisha]] (minor Rank), {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|qq=}} auxiliary shrine (*sessha*) | Established = Before [[Enryaku era|Enryaku]] 23 (804 CE)<ref name="sk7">Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): p. 237.</ref> | Main Hall = [[Shinmei-zukuri]] | Other Names = | festival = [[Kannamesai Festival|Kannamesai]] (Harvest Festival) | Rituals = }}
'''{{ill|Narahara Shrine|ja|奈良波良神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Narahara Shrine|qq=}}''' (*Narahara Jinja*) is an auxiliary shrine (*sessha*) of {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|qq=}} (the Inner Shrine of the [[Ise Grand Shrine]]), located in [[Tamaki]], [[Mie Prefecture]], Japan. It Ranks 22nd among the auxiliary shrines of the Inner Shrine.<ref>Ujiyamada City Office (1929): pp. 9–11.</ref>
The name "Narahara" means "oak plain," suggesting that the surrounding area was once a plain abundant with [[Nara tree|Nara]] (oak) trees.<ref name="ic">Ise Bunka Publishing (2008): p. 59.</ref><ref name="gk">Gakken Publishing (2013): p. 67.</ref>
== Overview == The shrine is located at Yagurado 833, Miyako, [[Tamaki]], [[Watarai District]], [[Mie Prefecture]], Japan.<ref name="tm">Tatsumi Publishing (2013): p. 48.</ref> It is situated on gently sloping terrain surrounded by privately owned forests to the east and west.<ref name="sk7"/> The shrine was established by {{ill|Yamato-hime-no-mikoto|ja|倭姫命|1=Yamatohime-no-mikoto|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato-hime-no-mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatohime-no-mikoto|qq=}}.<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk"/> The sacred object (*shintai*) of the shrine is a stone.<ref>Kaneko (1983): p. 59.</ref>
=== Shrine Name and location === Although officially named '''Narahara Shrine''' (*奈良波良神社*), it is referred to as '''Narahara Shrine''' (*楢原神社*) in the ''Ritual Records of the Imperial Grand Shrine'' (*皇太神宮儀式帳*).<ref name="sk7"/> The name suggests that the area was once covered with oak (*nara*) trees. The shrine is located in Miyako, which was the site of the "Miyako Misono," a sacred rice field (*shinden*) belonging to {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke Daijingu|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke Daijingu|qq=}} (the Outer Shrine of Ise).<ref name="ic"/>
According to the ''Seiyō Gorin Ikkyō'' (*勢陽五鈴遺響*), the name "Miyako" (meaning "capital") derives from an ancient plan to relocate the capital to this area. However, this plan was abandoned due to the emergence of Mt. Daibutsu (*大仏山*), leaving only the name to reflect the intended capital.<ref name="sk7"/> While the ''Report on the Investigation of Shikinaisha'' dismisses this as a "baseless theory," it acknowledges the connection between the shrine's name "'''Nara'''hara" and "Miyako" (都, meaning "capital").<ref name="sk7"/> The Tamaki Town Office explains that the old spelling of Miyako was "Miyako" (*宮子*), meaning "child of the shrine," symbolizing the blessings received from the deities.<ref>{{Cite web|language=ja|url=http://www.tamaki-kanko.jp/html/special_jingu-yukari.html|title=巡る神宮ゆかりの地 (Exploring Places Connected to the Grand Shrine)|publisher=Tamaki Town Office, Industrial Promotion Division|work=Nohon Tamaki Tabi|accessdate=2014-06-01|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20140601033540/http://www.tamaki-kanko.jp/html/special_jingu-yukari.html|archivedate=2014-06-01}}</ref>
== Grounds == The shrine grounds cover an area of 9,500 square meters.<ref name="sk9">Shikinaisha Research Association, 1990, p. 239.</ref> In ancient times, the grounds spanned approximately 5 chō (≈49,585 m²).<ref name="sk9"/> Although the shrine forest was confiscated during the 1871 [[Uchichiryo Edict]], 6,807 m² were returned in 1892.<ref name="sk9"/> The sacred grove is mainly composed of [[hinoki cypress]] trees, with some [[cedar]] and [[pine]] mixed in.<ref name="sk9"/>
Within the grounds, there is a natural stone [[temizuya]] (water basin) donated by devotees.<ref name="ic"/> A stone monument erected by the [[Kishu Domain]] prohibiting the killing of living beings remains on site.<ref name="gk"/>
The shrine pavilion, located near the center of the grounds, is constructed in the [[Shinmei-zukuri]] style with a [[board roof]]. It measures approximately 1.5 meters in length, 1.1 meters in width, and 2.9 meters in height.<ref name="sk0">Shikinaisha Research Association, 1990, p. 240.</ref> It faces south and is enclosed by a single layer of [[tamagaki]] fence with a [[Shinmei torii]] gate.<ref name="sk0"/>
== Enshrined deity == The enshrined deity is **Narahara Hime-no-Mikoto**.<ref name="ic"/> The deity is referred to as "Narawara Hime-no-Mikoto" in the *Ujiyamada City History*.<ref name="uy">Ujiyamada City Office, 1929, p. 11.</ref> She is considered the daughter of **Ōminakami-no-Mikoto**.<ref name="sk7"/>
Worshipped as the [[Ubusunagami]] (guardian deity) of the region,<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk"/> she is classified as a [[Kunitsukami]] (earthly deity).<ref name="tm"/> She is revered as the protector of rice field irrigation, sacred fields, and Narahara.<ref name="tm"/> According to tradition, she dedicated a sacred area to protect [[Yamato-hime-no-mikoto]] during her arduous journey.<ref name="tm"/>
== History == The shrine is believed to have been founded by Yamato-hime-no-mikoto, as mentioned in the *Kōtaijingu Gishiki-chō*.<ref name="sk7"/> Since this document dates back to 804 (Enryaku 23), the shrine's existence predates this period.<ref name="sk7"/> During the [[Medieval period]], the shrine fell into ruin until it was restored in 1663 (Kanbun 3) through the efforts of **Ōnakatomi Seichō (Kawabe Seichō)**.<ref>Shikinaisha Research Association, 1990, pp. 237, 240.</ref>
There is debate regarding the original shrine site, as the description in the *Kōtaijingu Gishiki-chō* does not align with the current location.<ref name="sk7"/> Some theories suggest the original site was deep in Miyako's mountains,<ref>Shikinaisha Research Association, 1990, pp. 237–238.</ref> while others propose it was at **Jōtō Shrine**.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Course 18: Exploring Satoyama Shrines|url=http://www.isekanko.jp/k-cycling/course18.html|publisher=Ise City Tourism Association|accessdate=2014-06-01}}</ref> Despite these theories, the shrine has remained at its current site since its restoration.<ref name="sk7"/>
The current shrine pavilion was rebuilt on October 8, 1980.<ref name="sk9"/>
== Rituals == Although there is no resident [[Kannushi]] (Shinto priest), one **Ikanushi** is appointed to manage daily affairs.<ref name="sk89">Shikinaisha Research Association, 1990, pp. 238–239.</ref>
Key rituals include: - **Kinensai (February 19)**: Prayer for abundant harvest - **Tsukinamisai (June 19 & December 19)**: Monthly festival - **Kannamesai (October 19)**: Offering of the first fruits to the deity - **Niinamesai (November 25)**: Celebration of the new harvest
Additional remote rituals are performed at the **Gojō-den Hall** of the Inner Shrine for New Year’s Day, National Foundation Day (February 11), and other imperial-related festivals.<ref name="sk89"/>
== Surroundings == The shrine is located west of the Miyako settlement, adjacent to the Hirose Seikō Tamaki Factory. It can be accessed via a gravel path after descending a slope.<ref>Ise Culture Publishing, 2008, p. 57.</ref> Nearby are several [[kofun]] (ancient Kofuns).<ref>Mie Prefecture Culture Promotion Division, 2012, p. 8.</ref> * **JR Sangu Line - Taki Station**: Approximately 35-minute walk<ref name="tm"/>. * **Ise Expressway - Tamaki IC**: About 2 minutes (830 m) by car.
== References ==
{{Reflist|2}}

ISBN 978-4-900759-37-4 * 宇治山田市役所 編『宇治山田市史 上巻』宇治山田市役所、昭和4th year 1月20日、862p. * [[学研パブリッシング]]『伊勢神宮に行こう』Gakken Mook神社紀行セレクションvol.1、[[薗田稔]]監修、学研マーケティング、2013rd year 7月4日、82p. ISBN 978-4-05-610047-1 * 金子延夫『玉城町史 第1巻―南伊勢の歴史と伝承―』三重県郷土資料叢書第61集、三重県郷土史料刊行会、昭和58th year 8月10日、276p. * 式内社研究会 編『式内社調査報告 第六巻 東海道1』[[Kogakkan University]]出版部、平成2nd year 2月28日、690p. ISBN 4-87644-080-8 * 三重県環境生活部文化振興課『熊野脇道 三重県 みえ歴史街道ウォーキングマップ』三重県環境生活部文化振興課、2012nd year * 『日本人なら知っておきたい 伊勢神宮と125の社』タツミムック、[[辰巳出版]]、2013rd year 11月1日、127p. ISBN 978-4-7778-1197-7 == See Also == * {{ill|List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|ja|伊勢国の式内社一覧|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|qq=}} * {{ill|List of 125 Shrines of Ise|ja|神宮125社の一覧|lt=List of 125 Shrines of Ise|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|3=伊勢国の式内社一覧|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of 125 Shrines of Ise|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|qq=}} * [[下外城田村]]

ISBN 4-87644-080-8 * 三重県環境生活部文化振興課『熊野脇道 三重県 みえ歴史街道ウォーキングマップ』三重県環境生活部文化振興課、2012nd year * 『日本人なら知っておきたい 伊勢神宮と125の社』タツミムック、[[辰巳出版]]、2013rd year 11月1日、127p.
