{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q17227047]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:28, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | Name = {{ill|Narahara Shrine|ja|奈良波良神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Narahara Shrine|qq=}} | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = File:Narahara-jinja.jpg | location = 833 Yagurado, Miyako, [[:en:Tamaki|Tamaki]], [[:en:Watarai District|Watarai District]], [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]], Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|34.471806|N|136.624861|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | Map = Japan Mie | deity = {{ill|Narahara-hime-no-mikoto|ja|那良原比女命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Narahara-hime-no-mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} | type = {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|qq=draft|lt=Shikinaisha}} (minor Rank), {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} auxiliary shrine (*sessha*) | Established = Before [[Enryaku era|Enryaku]] 23 (804 CE)<ref name="sk7">Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): p. 237.</ref> | Main Hall = [[Shinmei-zukuri]] | Other Names = | festival = [[Kannamesai Festival|Kannamesai]] (Harvest Festival) | Rituals = | location = {{ill|Tamaki|qid=Q587348}} | architecture_style = {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|qid=Q930545}} }}
'''{{ill|Narahara Shrine|ja|奈良波良神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Narahara Shrine|qq=}}''' (*Narahara Jinja*) is an auxiliary shrine (*sessha*) of {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (the Inner Shrine of the [[Ise Grand Shrine]]), located in [[:en:Tamaki|Tamaki]], [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]], Japan. It Ranks 22nd among the auxiliary shrines of the Inner Shrine.<ref>Ujiyamada City Office (1929): pp. 9–11.</ref>
The name "Narahara" means "oak plain," suggesting that the surrounding area was once a plain abundant with [[Nara tree|Nara]] (oak) trees.<ref name="ic">Ise Bunka Publishing (2008): p. 59.</ref><ref name="gk">Gakken Publishing (2013): p. 67.</ref>
== Overview == The shrine is located at Yagurado 833, Miyako, [[:en:Tamaki|Tamaki]], [[:en:Watarai District|Watarai District]], [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]], Japan.<ref name="tm">Tatsumi Publishing (2013): p. 48.</ref> It is situated on gently sloping terrain surrounded by privately owned forests to the east and west.<ref name="sk7"/> The shrine was established by [[:en:Yamatohime-no-mikoto|Yamatohime-no-mikoto]].<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk"/> The sacred object (*shintai*) of the shrine is a stone.<ref>Kaneko (1983): p. 59.</ref>
=== Shrine Name and location === Although officially named '''Narahara Shrine''' (*奈良波良神社*), it is referred to as '''Narahara Shrine''' (*楢原神社*) in the ''Ritual Records of the Imperial Grand Shrine'' (*皇太神宮儀式帳*).<ref name="sk7"/> The name suggests that the area was once covered with oak (*nara*) trees. The shrine is located in Miyako, which was the site of the "Miyako Misono," a sacred rice field (*shinden*) belonging to {{ill|Toyōke Daijingū|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyōke Daijingū|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (the Outer Shrine of Ise).<ref name="ic"/>
According to the ''Seiyō Gorin Ikkyō'' (*勢陽五鈴遺響*), the name "Miyako" (meaning "capital") derives from an ancient plan to relocate the capital to this area. However, this plan was abandoned due to the emergence of Mt. Daibutsu (*大仏山*), leaving only the name to reflect the intended capital.<ref name="sk7"/> While the ''Report on the Investigation of Shikinaisha'' dismisses this as a "baseless theory," it acknowledges the connection between the shrine's name "'''Nara'''hara" and "Miyako" (都, meaning "capital").<ref name="sk7"/> The Tamaki Town Office explains that the old spelling of Miyako was "Miyako" (*宮子*), meaning "child of the shrine," symbolizing the blessings received from the deities.<ref>{{Cite web|language=ja|url=http://www.tamaki-kanko.jp/html/special_jingu-yukari.html|title=巡る神宮ゆかりの地 (Exploring Places Connected to the Grand Shrine)|publisher=Tamaki Town Office, Industrial Promotion Division|work=Nohon Tamaki Tabi|accessdate=2014-06-01|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20140601033540/http://www.tamaki-kanko.jp/html/special_jingu-yukari.html|archivedate=2014-06-01}}</ref>
== Grounds == The shrine grounds cover an area of 9,500 square meters.<ref name="sk9">Shikinaisha Research Association, 1990, p. 239.</ref> In ancient times, the grounds spanned approximately 5 chō (≈49,585 m²).<ref name="sk9"/> Although the shrine forest was confiscated during the 1871 [[Uchichiryo Edict]], 6,807 m² were returned in 1892.<ref name="sk9"/> The sacred grove is mainly composed of [[:en:Hinoki cypress|Hinoki cypress]] trees, with some [[:en:Cedar|Cedar]] and [[:en:Pine|Pine]] mixed in.<ref name="sk9"/>
Within the grounds, there is a natural stone [[:en:Chōzuya|Chōzuya]] (water basin) donated by devotees.<ref name="ic"/> A stone monument erected by the [[:en:Kishu Domain|Kishu Domain]] prohibiting the killing of living beings remains on site.<ref name="gk"/>
The shrine pavilion, located near the center of the grounds, is constructed in the [[Shinmei-zukuri]] style with a [[:en:board roof|board roof]]. It measures approximately 1.5 meters in length, 1.1 meters in width, and 2.9 meters in height.<ref name="sk0">Shikinaisha Research Association, 1990, p. 240.</ref> It faces south and is enclosed by a single layer of [[:en:Tamagaki|Tamagaki]] fence with a [[Shinmei torii]] gate.<ref name="sk0"/>
== Enshrined deity == The enshrined deity is **Narahara Hime-no-Mikoto**.<ref name="ic"/> The deity is referred to as "Narawara Hime-no-Mikoto" in the *Ujiyamada City History*.<ref name="uy">Ujiyamada City Office, 1929, p. 11.</ref> She is considered the daughter of **Ōminakami-no-Mikoto**.<ref name="sk7"/>
Worshipped as the [[Ubusunagami]] (guardian deity) of the region,<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk"/> she is classified as a [[Kunitsukami]] (earthly deity).<ref name="tm"/> She is revered as the protector of rice field irrigation, sacred fields, and Narahara.<ref name="tm"/> According to tradition, she dedicated a sacred area to protect [[Yamato-hime-no-mikoto]] during her arduous journey.<ref name="tm"/>
== History == The shrine is believed to have been founded by Yamato-hime-no-mikoto, as mentioned in the *Kōtaijingu Gishiki-chō*.<ref name="sk7"/> Since this document dates back to 804 (Enryaku 23), the shrine's existence predates this period.<ref name="sk7"/> During the [[:en:Medieval period|Medieval period]], the shrine fell into ruin until it was restored in 1663 (Kanbun 3) through the efforts of **Ōnakatomi Seichō (Kawabe Seichō)**.<ref>Shikinaisha Research Association, 1990, pp. 237, 240.</ref>
There is debate regarding the original shrine site, as the description in the *Kōtaijingu Gishiki-chō* does not align with the current location.<ref name="sk7"/> Some theories suggest the original site was deep in Miyako's mountains,<ref>Shikinaisha Research Association, 1990, pp. 237–238.</ref> while others propose it was at **Jōtō Shrine**.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Course 18: Exploring Satoyama Shrines|url=http://www.isekanko.jp/k-cycling/course18.html|publisher=Ise City Tourism Association|accessdate=2014-06-01}}</ref> Despite these theories, the shrine has remained at its current site since its restoration.<ref name="sk7"/>
The current shrine pavilion was rebuilt on October 8, 1980.<ref name="sk9"/>
== Rituals == Although there is no resident [[Kannushi]] (Shinto priest), one **Ikanushi** is appointed to manage daily affairs.<ref name="sk89">Shikinaisha Research Association, 1990, pp. 238–239.</ref>
Key rituals include: - **Kinensai (February 19)**: Prayer for abundant harvest - **Tsukinamisai (June 19 & December 19)**: Monthly festival - **Kannamesai (October 19)**: Offering of the first fruits to the deity - **Niinamesai (November 25)**: Celebration of the new harvest
Additional remote rituals are performed at the **Gojō-den Hall** of the Inner Shrine for New Year’s Day, National Foundation Day (February 11), and other imperial-related festivals.<ref name="sk89"/>
== Surroundings == The shrine is located west of the Miyako settlement, adjacent to the Hirose Seikō Tamaki Factory. It can be accessed via a gravel path after descending a slope.<ref>Ise Culture Publishing, 2008, p. 57.</ref> Nearby are several [[kofun]] (ancient Kofuns).<ref>Mie Prefecture Culture Promotion Division, 2012, p. 8.</ref> * **JR Sangu Line - Taki Station**: Approximately 35-minute walk<ref name="tm"/>. * **Ise Expressway - Tamaki IC**: About 2 minutes (830 m) by car.
== References ==

ISBN 978-4-900759-37-4 * 宇治山田市役所 編『宇治山田市史 上巻』宇治山田市役所、昭和4th year 1月20日、862p. * [[:en:学研パブリッシング|学研パブリッシング]]『伊勢神宮に行こう』Gakken Mook神社紀行セレクションvol.1、[[薗田稔]]監修、学研マーケティング、2013rd year 7月4日、82p. ISBN 978-4-05-610047-1 * 金子延夫『玉城町史 第1巻―南伊勢の歴史と伝承―』三重県郷土資料叢書第61集、三重県郷土史料刊行会、昭和58th year 8月10日、276p. * 式内社研究会 編『式内社調査報告 第六巻 東海道1』[[:en:Kogakkan University|Kogakkan University]]出版部、平成2nd year 2月28日、690p. ISBN 4-87644-080-8 * 三重県環境生活部文化振興課『熊野脇道 三重県 みえ歴史街道ウォーキングマップ』三重県環境生活部文化振興課、2012nd year * 『日本人なら知っておきたい 伊勢神宮と125の社』タツミムック、[[辰巳出版]]、2013rd year 11月1日、127p. ISBN 978-4-7778-1197-7 == See Also == * {{ill|List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|ja|伊勢国の式内社一覧|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|qq=}} * {{ill|List of 125 Shrines of Ise|ja|神宮125社の一覧|lt=List of 125 Shrines of Ise|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|3=伊勢国の式内社一覧|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of 125 Shrines of Ise|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|qq=}} * [[下外城田村]]

The shrine is located in Miyako, which was the site of the "Miyako Misono," a sacred rice field (*shinden*) belonging to {{ill|Toyōke Daijingū|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyōke Daijingū|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (the Outer Shrine of Ise).<ref name="ic"/>
According to the ''Seiyō Gorin Ikkyō'' (*勢陽五鈴遺響*), the name "Miyako" (meaning "capital") derives from an ancient plan to relocate the capital to this area. However, this plan was abandoned due to the emergence of Mt. Daibutsu (*大仏山*), leaving only the name to reflect the intended capital.<ref name="sk7"/> While the ''Report on the Investigation of Shikinaisha'' dismisses this as a "baseless theory," it acknowledges the connection between the shrine's name "'''Nara'''hara" and "Miyako" (都, meaning "capital").<ref name="sk7"/> The Tamaki Town Office explains that the old spelling of Miyako was "Miyako" (*宮子*), meaning "child of the shrine," symbolizing the blessings received from the deities.<ref>{{Cite web|language=ja|url=http://www.tamaki-kanko.jp/html/special_jingu-yukari.html|title=巡る神宮ゆかりの地 (Exploring Places Connected to the Grand Shrine)|publisher=Tamaki Town Office, Industrial Promotion Division|work=Nohon Tamaki Tabi|accessdate=2014-06-01|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20140601033540/http://www.tamaki-kanko.jp/html/special_jingu-yukari.html|archivedate=2014-06-01}}</ref>
== Grounds == The shrine grounds cover an area of 9,500 square meters.<ref name="sk9">Shikinaisha Research Association, 1990, p. 239.</ref> In ancient times, the grounds spanned approximately 5 chō (≈49,585 m²).<ref name="sk9"/> Although the shrine forest was confiscated during the 1871 [[Uchichiryo Edict]], 6,807 m² were returned in 1892.<ref name="sk9"/> The sacred grove is mainly composed of [[:en:Hinoki cypress|Hinoki cypress]] trees, with some [[:en:Cedar|Cedar]] and [[:en:Pine|Pine]] mixed in.<ref name="sk9"/>
Within the grounds, there is a natural stone [[:en:Chōzuya|Chōzuya]] (water basin) donated by devotees.<ref name="ic"/> A stone monument erected by the [[:en:Kishu Domain|Kishu Domain]] prohibiting the killing of living beings remains on site.<ref name="gk"/>
The shrine pavilion, located near the center of the grounds, is constructed in the [[Shinmei-zukuri]] style with a [[:en:board roof|board roof]]. It measures approximately 1.5 meters in length, 1.1 meters in width, and 2.9 meters in height.<ref name="sk0">Shikinaisha Research Association, 1990, p. 240.</ref> It faces south and is enclosed by a single layer of [[:en:Tamagaki|Tamagaki]] fence with a [[Shinmei torii]] gate.<ref name="sk0"/>
== Enshrined deity == The enshrined deity is **Narahara Hime-no-Mikoto**.<ref name="ic"/> The deity is referred to as "Narawara Hime-no-Mikoto" in the *Ujiyamada City History*.<ref name="uy">Ujiyamada City Office, 1929, p. 11.</ref> She is considered the daughter of **Ōminakami-no-Mikoto**.<ref name="sk7"/>
Worshipped as the [[Ubusunagami]] (guardian deity) of the region,<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk"/> she is classified as a [[Kunitsukami]] (earthly deity).<ref name="tm"/> She is revered as the protector of rice field irrigation, sacred fields, and Narahara.<ref name="tm"/> According to tradition, she dedicated a sacred area to protect [[Yamato-hime-no-mikoto]] during her arduous journey.<ref name="tm"/>
== History == The shrine is believed to have been founded by Yamato-hime-no-mikoto, as mentioned in the *Kōtaijingu Gishiki-chō*.<ref name="sk7"/> Since this document dates back to 804 (Enryaku 23), the shrine's existence predates this period.<ref name="sk7"/> During the [[:en:Medieval period|Medieval period]], the shrine fell into ruin until it was restored in 1663 (Kanbun 3) through the efforts of **Ōnakatomi Seichō (Kawabe Seichō)**.<ref>Shikinaisha Research Association, 1990, pp. 237, 240.</ref>
There is debate regarding the original shrine site, as the description in the *Kōtaijingu Gishiki-chō* does not align with the current location.<ref name="sk7"/> Some theories suggest the original site was deep in Miyako's mountains,<ref>Shikinaisha Research Association, 1990, pp. 237–238.</ref> while others propose it was at **Jōtō Shrine**.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Course 18: Exploring Satoyama Shrines|url=http://www.isekanko.jp/k-cycling/course18.html|publisher=Ise City Tourism Association|accessdate=2014-06-01}}</ref> Despite these theories, the shrine has remained at its current site since its restoration.<ref name="sk7"/>
The current shrine pavilion was rebuilt on October 8, 1980.<ref name="sk9"/>
== Rituals == Although there is no resident [[Kannushi]] (Shinto priest), one **Ikanushi** is appointed to manage daily affairs.<ref name="sk89">Shikinaisha Research Association, 1990, pp. 238–239.</ref>
Key rituals include: - **Kinensai (February 19)**: Prayer for abundant harvest - **Tsukinamisai (June 19 & December 19)**: Monthly festival - **Kannamesai (October 19)**: Offering of the first fruits to the deity - **Niinamesai (November 25)**: Celebration of the new harvest
Additional remote rituals are performed at the **Gojō-den Hall** of the Inner Shrine for New Year’s Day, National Foundation Day (February 11), and other imperial-related festivals.<ref name="sk89"/>
== Surroundings == The shrine is located west of the Miyako settlement, adjacent to the Hirose Seikō Tamaki Factory. It can be accessed via a gravel path after descending a slope.<ref>Ise Culture Publishing, 2008, p. 57.</ref> Nearby are several [[kofun]] (ancient Kofuns).<ref>Mie Prefecture Culture Promotion Division, 2012, p. 8.</ref> * **JR Sangu Line - Taki Station**: Approximately 35-minute walk<ref name="tm"/>. * **Ise Expressway - Tamaki IC**: About 2 minutes (830 m) by car.
== References ==
{{Reflist|2}}

ISBN 978-4-900759-37-4 * 宇治山田市役所 編『宇治山田市史 上巻』宇治山田市役所、昭和4th year 1月20日、862p. * [[:en:学研パブリッシング|学研パブリッシング]]『伊勢神宮に行こう』Gakken Mook神社紀行セレクションvol.1、[[薗田稔]]監修、学研マーケティング、2013rd year 7月4日、82p. ISBN 978-4-05-610047-1 * 金子延夫『玉城町史 第1巻―南伊勢の歴史と伝承―』三重県郷土資料叢書第61集、三重県郷土史料刊行会、昭和58th year 8月10日、276p. * 式内社研究会 編『式内社調査報告 第六巻 東海道1』[[:en:Kogakkan University|Kogakkan University]]出版部、平成2nd year 2月28日、690p. ISBN 4-87644-080-8 * 三重県環境生活部文化振興課『熊野脇道 三重県 みえ歴史街道ウォーキングマップ』三重県環境生活部文化振興課、2012nd year * 『日本人なら知っておきたい 伊勢神宮と125の社』タツミムック、[[辰巳出版]]、2013rd year 11月1日、127p. ISBN 978-4-7778-1197-7 == See Also == * {{ill|List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|ja|伊勢国の式内社一覧|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|qq=}} * {{ill|List of 125 Shrines of Ise|ja|神宮125社の一覧|lt=List of 125 Shrines of Ise|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|3=伊勢国の式内社一覧|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of 125 Shrines of Ise|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|qq=}} * [[下外城田村]]

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q17227047]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:28, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | Name = {{ill|Narahara Shrine|ja|奈良波良神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Narahara Shrine|qq=}} | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = File:Narahara-jinja.jpg | location = 833 Yagurado, Miyako, [[:en:Tamaki|Tamaki]], [[:en:Watarai District|Watarai District]], [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]], Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|34.471806|N|136.624861|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | Map = Japan Mie | deity = {{ill|Narahara-hime-no-mikoto|ja|那良原比女命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Narahara-hime-no-mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} | type = {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|qq=draft|lt=Shikinaisha}} (minor Rank), {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} auxiliary shrine (*sessha*) | Established = Before [[Enryaku era|Enryaku]] 23 (804 CE)<ref name="sk7">Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): p. 237.</ref> | Main Hall = [[Shinmei-zukuri]] | Other Names = | festival = [[Kannamesai Festival|Kannamesai]] (Harvest Festival) | Rituals = | location = {{ill|Tamaki|qid=Q587348}} | architecture_style = {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|qid=Q930545}} }}
'''{{ill|Narahara Shrine|ja|奈良波良神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Narahara Shrine|qq=}}''' (*Narahara Jinja*) is an auxiliary shrine (*sessha*) of {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (the Inner Shrine of the [[Ise Grand Shrine]]), located in [[:en:Tamaki|Tamaki]], [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]], Japan. It Ranks 22nd among the auxiliary shrines of the Inner Shrine.<ref>Ujiyamada City Office (1929): pp. 9–11.</ref>
The name "Narahara" means "oak plain," suggesting that the surrounding area was once a plain abundant with [[Nara tree|Nara]] (oak) trees.<ref name="ic">Ise Bunka Publishing (2008): p. 59.</ref><ref name="gk">Gakken Publishing (2013): p. 67.</ref>
== Overview == The shrine is located at Yagurado 833, Miyako, [[:en:Tamaki|Tamaki]], [[:en:Watarai District|Watarai District]], [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]], Japan.<ref name="tm">Tatsumi Publishing (2013): p. 48.</ref> It is situated on gently sloping terrain surrounded by privately owned forests to the east and west.<ref name="sk7"/> The shrine was established by [[:en:Yamatohime-no-mikoto|Yamatohime-no-mikoto]].<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk"/> The sacred object (*shintai*) of the shrine is a stone.<ref>Kaneko (1983): p. 59.</ref>
=== Shrine Name and location === Although officially named '''Narahara Shrine''' (*奈良波良神社*), it is referred to as '''Narahara Shrine''' (*楢原神社*) in the ''Ritual Records of the Imperial Grand Shrine'' (*皇太神宮儀式帳*).<ref name="sk7"/> The name suggests that the area was once covered with oak (*nara*) trees. The shrine is located in Miyako, which was the site of the "Miyako Misono," a sacred rice field (*shinden*) belonging to {{ill|Toyōke Daijingū|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyōke Daijingū|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (the Outer Shrine of Ise).<ref name="ic"/>
According to the ''Seiyō Gorin Ikkyō'' (*勢陽五鈴遺響*), the name "Miyako" (meaning "capital") derives from an ancient plan to relocate the capital to this area. However, this plan was abandoned due to the emergence of Mt. Daibutsu (*大仏山*), leaving only the name to reflect the intended capital.<ref name="sk7"/> While the ''Report on the Investigation of Shikinaisha'' dismisses this as a "baseless theory," it acknowledges the connection between the shrine's name "'''Nara'''hara" and "Miyako" (都, meaning "capital").<ref name="sk7"/> The Tamaki Town Office explains that the old spelling of Miyako was "Miyako" (*宮子*), meaning "child of the shrine," symbolizing the blessings received from the deities.<ref>{{Cite web|language=ja|url=http://www.tamaki-kanko.jp/html/special_jingu-yukari.html|title=巡る神宮ゆかりの地 (Exploring Places Connected to the Grand Shrine)|publisher=Tamaki Town Office, Industrial Promotion Division|work=Nohon Tamaki Tabi|accessdate=2014-06-01|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20140601033540/http://www.tamaki-kanko.jp/html/special_jingu-yukari.html|archivedate=2014-06-01}}</ref>
== Grounds == The shrine grounds cover an area of 9,500 square meters.<ref name="sk9">Shikinaisha Research Association, 1990, p. 239.</ref> In ancient times, the grounds spanned approximately 5 chō (≈49,585 m²).<ref name="sk9"/> Although the shrine forest was confiscated during the 1871 [[Uchichiryo Edict]], 6,807 m² were returned in 1892.<ref name="sk9"/> The sacred grove is mainly composed of [[:en:Hinoki cypress|Hinoki cypress]] trees, with some [[:en:Cedar|Cedar]] and [[:en:Pine|Pine]] mixed in.<ref name="sk9"/>
Within the grounds, there is a natural stone [[:en:Chōzuya|Chōzuya]] (water basin) donated by devotees.<ref name="ic"/> A stone monument erected by the [[:en:Kishu Domain|Kishu Domain]] prohibiting the killing of living beings remains on site.<ref name="gk"/>
The shrine pavilion, located near the center of the grounds, is constructed in the [[Shinmei-zukuri]] style with a [[:en:board roof|board roof]]. It measures approximately 1.5 meters in length, 1.1 meters in width, and 2.9 meters in height.<ref name="sk0">Shikinaisha Research Association, 1990, p. 240.</ref> It faces south and is enclosed by a single layer of [[:en:Tamagaki|Tamagaki]] fence with a [[Shinmei torii]] gate.<ref name="sk0"/>
== Enshrined deity == The enshrined deity is **Narahara Hime-no-Mikoto**.<ref name="ic"/> The deity is referred to as "Narawara Hime-no-Mikoto" in the *Ujiyamada City History*.<ref name="uy">Ujiyamada City Office, 1929, p. 11.</ref> She is considered the daughter of **Ōminakami-no-Mikoto**.<ref name="sk7"/>
Worshipped as the [[Ubusunagami]] (guardian deity) of the region,<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk"/> she is classified as a [[Kunitsukami]] (earthly deity).<ref name="tm"/> She is revered as the protector of rice field irrigation, sacred fields, and Narahara.<ref name="tm"/> According to tradition, she dedicated a sacred area to protect [[Yamato-hime-no-mikoto]] during her arduous journey.<ref name="tm"/>
== History == The shrine is believed to have been founded by Yamato-hime-no-mikoto, as mentioned in the *Kōtaijingu Gishiki-chō*.<ref name="sk7"/> Since this document dates back to 804 (Enryaku 23), the shrine's existence predates this period.<ref name="sk7"/> During the [[:en:Medieval period|Medieval period]], the shrine fell into ruin until it was restored in 1663 (Kanbun 3) through the efforts of **Ōnakatomi Seichō (Kawabe Seichō)**.<ref>Shikinaisha Research Association, 1990, pp. 237, 240.</ref>
There is debate regarding the original shrine site, as the description in the *Kōtaijingu Gishiki-chō* does not align with the current location.<ref name="sk7"/> Some theories suggest the original site was deep in Miyako's mountains,<ref>Shikinaisha Research Association, 1990, pp. 237–238.</ref> while others propose it was at **Jōtō Shrine**.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Course 18: Exploring Satoyama Shrines|url=http://www.isekanko.jp/k-cycling/course18.html|publisher=Ise City Tourism Association|accessdate=2014-06-01}}</ref> Despite these theories, the shrine has remained at its current site since its restoration.<ref name="sk7"/>
The current shrine pavilion was rebuilt on October 8, 1980.<ref name="sk9"/>
== Rituals == Although there is no resident [[Kannushi]] (Shinto priest), one **Ikanushi** is appointed to manage daily affairs.<ref name="sk89">Shikinaisha Research Association, 1990, pp. 238–239.</ref>
Key rituals include: - **Kinensai (February 19)**: Prayer for abundant harvest - **Tsukinamisai (June 19 & December 19)**: Monthly festival - **Kannamesai (October 19)**: Offering of the first fruits to the deity - **Niinamesai (November 25)**: Celebration of the new harvest
Additional remote rituals are performed at the **Gojō-den Hall** of the Inner Shrine for New Year’s Day, National Foundation Day (February 11), and other imperial-related festivals.<ref name="sk89"/>
== Surroundings == The shrine is located west of the Miyako settlement, adjacent to the Hirose Seikō Tamaki Factory. It can be accessed via a gravel path after descending a slope.<ref>Ise Culture Publishing, 2008, p. 57.</ref> Nearby are several [[kofun]] (ancient Kofuns).<ref>Mie Prefecture Culture Promotion Division, 2012, p. 8.</ref> * **JR Sangu Line - Taki Station**: Approximately 35-minute walk<ref name="tm"/>. * **Ise Expressway - Tamaki IC**: About 2 minutes (830 m) by car.

'''{{ill|Narahara Shrine|ja|奈良波良神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Narahara Shrine|qq=}}''' (*Narahara Jinja*) is an auxiliary shrine (*sessha*) of {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (the Inner Shrine of the [[Ise Grand Shrine]]), located in [[:en:Tamaki|Tamaki]], [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]], Japan. It Ranks 22nd among the auxiliary shrines of the Inner Shrine.<ref>Ujiyamada City Office (1929): pp. 9–11.</ref>
The name "Narahara" means "oak plain," suggesting that the surrounding area was once a plain abundant with [[Nara tree|Nara]] (oak) trees.<ref name="ic">Ise Bunka Publishing (2008): p. 59.</ref><ref name="gk">Gakken Publishing (2013): p. 67.</ref>
== Overview == The shrine is located at Yagurado 833, Miyako, [[:en:Tamaki|Tamaki]], [[:en:Watarai District|Watarai District]], [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]], Japan.<ref name="tm">Tatsumi Publishing (2013): p. 48.</ref> It is situated on gently sloping terrain surrounded by privately owned forests to the east and west.<ref name="sk7"/> The shrine was established by [[:en:Yamatohime-no-mikoto|Yamatohime-no-mikoto]].<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk"/> The sacred object (*shintai*) of the shrine is a stone.<ref>Kaneko (1983): p. 59.</ref>
=== Shrine Name and location === Although officially named '''Narahara Shrine''' (*奈良波良神社*), it is referred to as '''Narahara Shrine''' (*楢原神社*) in the ''Ritual Records of the Imperial Grand Shrine'' (*皇太神宮儀式帳*).<ref name="sk7"/> The name suggests that the area was once covered with oak (*nara*) trees. The shrine is located in Miyako, which was the site of the "Miyako Misono," a sacred rice field (*shinden*) belonging to {{ill|Toyōke Daijingū|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyōke Daijingū|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (the Outer Shrine of Ise).<ref name="ic"/>
According to the ''Seiyō Gorin Ikkyō'' (*勢陽五鈴遺響*), the name "Miyako" (meaning "capital") derives from an ancient plan to relocate the capital to this area. However, this plan was abandoned due to the emergence of Mt. Daibutsu (*大仏山*), leaving only the name to reflect the intended capital.<ref name="sk7"/> While the ''Report on the Investigation of Shikinaisha'' dismisses this as a "baseless theory," it acknowledges the connection between the shrine's name "'''Nara'''hara" and "Miyako" (都, meaning "capital").<ref name="sk7"/> The Tamaki Town Office explains that the old spelling of Miyako was "Miyako" (*宮子*), meaning "child of the shrine," symbolizing the blessings received from the deities.<ref>{{Cite web|language=ja|url=http://www.tamaki-kanko.jp/html/special_jingu-yukari.html|title=巡る神宮ゆかりの地 (Exploring Places Connected to the Grand Shrine)|publisher=Tamaki Town Office, Industrial Promotion Division|work=Nohon Tamaki Tabi|accessdate=2014-06-01|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20140601033540/http://www.tamaki-kanko.jp/html/special_jingu-yukari.html|archivedate=2014-06-01}}</ref>
== Grounds == The shrine grounds cover an area of 9,500 square meters.<ref name="sk9">Shikinaisha Research Association, 1990, p. 239.</ref> In ancient times, the grounds spanned approximately 5 chō (≈49,585 m²).<ref name="sk9"/> Although the shrine forest was confiscated during the 1871 [[Uchichiryo Edict]], 6,807 m² were returned in 1892.<ref name="sk9"/> The sacred grove is mainly composed of [[:en:Hinoki cypress|Hinoki cypress]] trees, with some [[:en:Cedar|Cedar]] and [[:en:Pine|Pine]] mixed in.<ref name="sk9"/>
Within the grounds, there is a natural stone [[:en:Chōzuya|Chōzuya]] (water basin) donated by devotees.<ref name="ic"/> A stone monument erected by the [[:en:Kishu Domain|Kishu Domain]] prohibiting the killing of living beings remains on site.<ref name="gk"/>
The shrine pavilion, located near the center of the grounds, is constructed in the [[Shinmei-zukuri]] style with a [[:en:board roof|board roof]]. It measures approximately 1.5 meters in length, 1.1 meters in width, and 2.9 meters in height.<ref name="sk0">Shikinaisha Research Association, 1990, p. 240.</ref> It faces south and is enclosed by a single layer of [[:en:Tamagaki|Tamagaki]] fence with a [[Shinmei torii]] gate.<ref name="sk0"/>
== Enshrined deity == The enshrined deity is **Narahara Hime-no-Mikoto**.<ref name="ic"/> The deity is referred to as "Narawara Hime-no-Mikoto" in the *Ujiyamada City History*.<ref name="uy">Ujiyamada City Office, 1929, p. 11.</ref> She is considered the daughter of **Ōminakami-no-Mikoto**.<ref name="sk7"/>
Worshipped as the [[Ubusunagami]] (guardian deity) of the region,<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk"/> she is classified as a [[Kunitsukami]] (earthly deity).<ref name="tm"/> She is revered as the protector of rice field irrigation, sacred fields, and Narahara.<ref name="tm"/> According to tradition, she dedicated a sacred area to protect [[Yamato-hime-no-mikoto]] during her arduous journey.<ref name="tm"/>
== History == The shrine is believed to have been founded by Yamato-hime-no-mikoto, as mentioned in the *Kōtaijingu Gishiki-chō*.<ref name="sk7"/> Since this document dates back to 804 (Enryaku 23), the shrine's existence predates this period.<ref name="sk7"/> During the [[:en:Medieval period|Medieval period]], the shrine fell into ruin until it was restored in 1663 (Kanbun 3) through the efforts of **Ōnakatomi Seichō (Kawabe Seichō)**.<ref>Shikinaisha Research Association, 1990, pp. 237, 240.</ref>
There is debate regarding the original shrine site, as the description in the *Kōtaijingu Gishiki-chō* does not align with the current location.<ref name="sk7"/> Some theories suggest the original site was deep in Miyako's mountains,<ref>Shikinaisha Research Association, 1990, pp. 237–238.</ref> while others propose it was at **Jōtō Shrine**.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Course 18: Exploring Satoyama Shrines|url=http://www.isekanko.jp/k-cycling/course18.html|publisher=Ise City Tourism Association|accessdate=2014-06-01}}</ref> Despite these theories, the shrine has remained at its current site since its restoration.<ref name="sk7"/>
The current shrine pavilion was rebuilt on October 8, 1980.<ref name="sk9"/>
== Rituals == Although there is no resident [[Kannushi]] (Shinto priest), one **Ikanushi** is appointed to manage daily affairs.<ref name="sk89">Shikinaisha Research Association, 1990, pp. 238–239.</ref>
Key rituals include: - **Kinensai (February 19)**: Prayer for abundant harvest - **Tsukinamisai (June 19 & December 19)**: Monthly festival - **Kannamesai (October 19)**: Offering of the first fruits to the deity - **Niinamesai (November 25)**: Celebration of the new harvest
Additional remote rituals are performed at the **Gojō-den Hall** of the Inner Shrine for New Year’s Day, National Foundation Day (February 11), and other imperial-related festivals.<ref name="sk89"/>
== Surroundings == The shrine is located west of the Miyako settlement, adjacent to the Hirose Seikō Tamaki Factory. It can be accessed via a gravel path after descending a slope.<ref>Ise Culture Publishing, 2008, p. 57.</ref> Nearby are several [[kofun]] (ancient Kofuns).<ref>Mie Prefecture Culture Promotion Division, 2012, p. 8.</ref> * **JR Sangu Line - Taki Station**: Approximately 35-minute walk<ref name="tm"/>. * **Ise Expressway - Tamaki IC**: About 2 minutes (830 m) by car.
== References ==
{{Reflist|2}}
== Bibliography == * [[伊勢文化舎]] 編『お伊勢さん125社めぐり』別冊『伊勢人』、伊勢文化舎、平成20th year 12月23日、151p. ISBN 978-4-900759-37-4 * 宇治山田市役所 編『宇治山田市史 上巻』宇治山田市役所、昭和4th year 1月20日、862p. * [[:en:学研パブリッシング|学研パブリッシング]]『伊勢神宮に行こう』Gakken Mook神社紀行セレクションvol.1、[[薗田稔]]監修、学研マーケティング、2013rd year 7月4日、82p. ISBN 978-4-05-610047-1 * 金子延夫『玉城町史 第1巻―南伊勢の歴史と伝承―』三重県郷土資料叢書第61集、三重県郷土史料刊行会、昭和58th year 8月10日、276p. * 式内社研究会 編『式内社調査報告 第六巻 東海道1』[[:en:Kogakkan University|Kogakkan University]]出版部、平成2nd year 2月28日、690p. ISBN 4-87644-080-8 * 三重県環境生活部文化振興課『熊野脇道 三重県 みえ歴史街道ウォーキングマップ』三重県環境生活部文化振興課、2012nd year * 『日本人なら知っておきたい 伊勢神宮と125の社』タツミムック、[[辰巳出版]]、2013rd year 11月1日、127p. ISBN 978-4-7778-1197-7 == See Also == * {{ill|List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|ja|伊勢国の式内社一覧|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|qq=}} * {{ill|List of 125 Shrines of Ise|ja|神宮125社の一覧|lt=List of 125 Shrines of Ise|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|3=伊勢国の式内社一覧|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of 125 Shrines of Ise|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|qq=}} * [[下外城田村]]
== External Links == {{ external media | align = | width = 230px | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image1 = [http://www.sengukan.jp/wp-content/themes/sengukan/images/shrine/area04/area04_04.jpg 奈良波良神社] }}
* [http://www.jingukaikan.jp/125mairi/m08/0803.html 奈良波良神社（ならはらじんじゃ）] - 一般財団法人伊勢神宮崇敬会 * [http://www.town.tamaki.mie.jp/hpdata/kanbun/jinjya.html 神社めぐり] - 玉城町

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q17227047]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:28, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | Name = {{ill|Narahara Shrine|ja|奈良波良神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Narahara Shrine|qq=}} | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = File:Narahara-jinja.jpg | location = 833 Yagurado, Miyako, [[:en:Tamaki|Tamaki]], [[:en:Watarai District|Watarai District]], [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]], Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|34.471806|N|136.624861|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | Map = Japan Mie | deity = {{ill|Narahara-hime-no-mikoto|ja|那良原比女命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Narahara-hime-no-mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} | type = {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|qq=draft|lt=Shikinaisha}} (minor Rank), {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} auxiliary shrine (*sessha*) | Established = Before [[Enryaku era|Enryaku]] 23 (804 CE)<ref name="sk7">Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): p. 237.</ref> | Main Hall = [[Shinmei-zukuri]] | Other Names = | festival = [[Kannamesai Festival|Kannamesai]] (Harvest Festival) | Rituals = | location = {{ill|Tamaki|qid=Q587348}} | architecture_style = {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|qid=Q930545}} }}
'''{{ill|Narahara Shrine|ja|奈良波良神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Narahara Shrine|qq=}}''' (*Narahara Jinja*) is an auxiliary shrine (*sessha*) of {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (the Inner Shrine of the [[Ise Grand Shrine]]), located in [[:en:Tamaki|Tamaki]], [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]], Japan. It Ranks 22nd among the auxiliary shrines of the Inner Shrine.<ref>Ujiyamada City Office (1929): pp. 9–11.</ref>
The name "Narahara" means "oak plain," suggesting that the surrounding area was once a plain abundant with [[Nara tree|Nara]] (oak) trees.<ref name="ic">Ise Bunka Publishing (2008): p. 59.</ref><ref name="gk">Gakken Publishing (2013): p. 67.</ref>
== Overview == The shrine is located at Yagurado 833, Miyako, [[:en:Tamaki|Tamaki]], [[:en:Watarai District|Watarai District]], [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]], Japan.<ref name="tm">Tatsumi Publishing (2013): p. 48.</ref> It is situated on gently sloping terrain surrounded by privately owned forests to the east and west.<ref name="sk7"/> The shrine was established by [[:en:Yamatohime-no-mikoto|Yamatohime-no-mikoto]].<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk"/> The sacred object (*shintai*) of the shrine is a stone.<ref>Kaneko (1983): p. 59.</ref>
=== Shrine Name and location === Although officially named '''Narahara Shrine''' (*奈良波良神社*), it is referred to as '''Narahara Shrine''' (*楢原神社*) in the ''Ritual Records of the Imperial Grand Shrine'' (*皇太神宮儀式帳*).<ref name="sk7"/> The name suggests that the area was once covered with oak (*nara*) trees. The shrine is located in Miyako, which was the site of the "Miyako Misono," a sacred rice field (*shinden*) belonging to {{ill|Toyōke Daijingū|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyōke Daijingū|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (the Outer Shrine of Ise).<ref name="ic"/>
According to the ''Seiyō Gorin Ikkyō'' (*勢陽五鈴遺響*), the name "Miyako" (meaning "capital") derives from an ancient plan to relocate the capital to this area. However, this plan was abandoned due to the emergence of Mt. Daibutsu (*大仏山*), leaving only the name to reflect the intended capital.<ref name="sk7"/> While the ''Report on the Investigation of Shikinaisha'' dismisses this as a "baseless theory," it acknowledges the connection between the shrine's name "'''Nara'''hara" and "Miyako" (都, meaning "capital").<ref name="sk7"/> The Tamaki Town Office explains that the old spelling of Miyako was "Miyako" (*宮子*), meaning "child of the shrine," symbolizing the blessings received from the deities.<ref>{{Cite web|language=ja|url=http://www.tamaki-kanko.jp/html/special_jingu-yukari.html|title=巡る神宮ゆかりの地 (Exploring Places Connected to the Grand Shrine)|publisher=Tamaki Town Office, Industrial Promotion Division|work=Nohon Tamaki Tabi|accessdate=2014-06-01|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20140601033540/http://www.tamaki-kanko.jp/html/special_jingu-yukari.html|archivedate=2014-06-01}}</ref>
== Grounds == The shrine grounds cover an area of 9,500 square meters.<ref name="sk9">Shikinaisha Research Association, 1990, p. 239.</ref> In ancient times, the grounds spanned approximately 5 chō (≈49,585 m²).<ref name="sk9"/> Although the shrine forest was confiscated during the 1871 [[Uchichiryo Edict]], 6,807 m² were returned in 1892.<ref name="sk9"/> The sacred grove is mainly composed of [[:en:Hinoki cypress|Hinoki cypress]] trees, with some [[:en:Cedar|Cedar]] and [[:en:Pine|Pine]] mixed in.<ref name="sk9"/>
Within the grounds, there is a natural stone [[:en:Chōzuya|Chōzuya]] (water basin) donated by devotees.<ref name="ic"/> A stone monument erected by the [[:en:Kishu Domain|Kishu Domain]] prohibiting the killing of living beings remains on site.<ref name="gk"/>
The shrine pavilion, located near the center of the grounds, is constructed in the [[Shinmei-zukuri]] style with a [[:en:board roof|board roof]]. It measures approximately 1.5 meters in length, 1.1 meters in width, and 2.9 meters in height.<ref name="sk0">Shikinaisha Research Association, 1990, p. 240.</ref> It faces south and is enclosed by a single layer of [[:en:Tamagaki|Tamagaki]] fence with a [[Shinmei torii]] gate.<ref name="sk0"/>
== Enshrined deity == The enshrined deity is **Narahara Hime-no-Mikoto**.<ref name="ic"/> The deity is referred to as "Narawara Hime-no-Mikoto" in the *Ujiyamada City History*.<ref name="uy">Ujiyamada City Office, 1929, p. 11.</ref> She is considered the daughter of **Ōminakami-no-Mikoto**.<ref name="sk7"/>
Worshipped as the [[Ubusunagami]] (guardian deity) of the region,<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk"/> she is classified as a [[Kunitsukami]] (earthly deity).<ref name="tm"/> She is revered as the protector of rice field irrigation, sacred fields, and Narahara.<ref name="tm"/> According to tradition, she dedicated a sacred area to protect [[Yamato-hime-no-mikoto]] during her arduous journey.<ref name="tm"/>
== History == The shrine is believed to have been founded by Yamato-hime-no-mikoto, as mentioned in the *Kōtaijingu Gishiki-chō*.<ref name="sk7"/> Since this document dates back to 804 (Enryaku 23), the shrine's existence predates this period.<ref name="sk7"/> During the [[:en:Medieval period|Medieval period]], the shrine fell into ruin until it was restored in 1663 (Kanbun 3) through the efforts of **Ōnakatomi Seichō (Kawabe Seichō)**.<ref>Shikinaisha Research Association, 1990, pp. 237, 240.</ref>
There is debate regarding the original shrine site, as the description in the *Kōtaijingu Gishiki-chō* does not align with the current location.<ref name="sk7"/> Some theories suggest the original site was deep in Miyako's mountains,<ref>Shikinaisha Research Association, 1990, pp. 237–238.</ref> while others propose it was at **Jōtō Shrine**.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Course 18: Exploring Satoyama Shrines|url=http://www.isekanko.jp/k-cycling/course18.html|publisher=Ise City Tourism Association|accessdate=2014-06-01}}</ref> Despite these theories, the shrine has remained at its current site since its restoration.<ref name="sk7"/>
The current shrine pavilion was rebuilt on October 8, 1980.<ref name="sk9"/>
== Rituals == Although there is no resident [[Kannushi]] (Shinto priest), one **Ikanushi** is appointed to manage daily affairs.<ref name="sk89">Shikinaisha Research Association, 1990, pp. 238–239.</ref>
Key rituals include: - **Kinensai (February 19)**: Prayer for abundant harvest - **Tsukinamisai (June 19 & December 19)**: Monthly festival - **Kannamesai (October 19)**: Offering of the first fruits to the deity - **Niinamesai (November 25)**: Celebration of the new harvest
Additional remote rituals are performed at the **Gojō-den Hall** of the Inner Shrine for New Year’s Day, National Foundation Day (February 11), and other imperial-related festivals.<ref name="sk89"/>
== Surroundings == The shrine is located west of the Miyako settlement, adjacent to the Hirose Seikō Tamaki Factory. It can be accessed via a gravel path after descending a slope.<ref>Ise Culture Publishing, 2008, p. 57.</ref> Nearby are several [[kofun]] (ancient Kofuns).<ref>Mie Prefecture Culture Promotion Division, 2012, p. 8.</ref> * **JR Sangu Line - Taki Station**: Approximately 35-minute walk<ref name="tm"/>. * **Ise Expressway - Tamaki IC**: About 2 minutes (830 m) by car.
== References ==
{{Reflist|2}}
== Bibliography == * [[伊勢文化舎]] 編『お伊勢さん125社めぐり』別冊『伊勢人』、伊勢文化舎、平成20th year 12月23日、151p. ISBN 978-4-900759-37-4 * 宇治山田市役所 編『宇治山田市史 上巻』宇治山田市役所、昭和4th year 1月20日、862p. * [[:en:学研パブリッシング|学研パブリッシング]]『伊勢神宮に行こう』Gakken Mook神社紀行セレクションvol.1、[[薗田稔]]監修、学研マーケティング、2013rd year 7月4日、82p. ISBN 978-4-05-610047-1 * 金子延夫『玉城町史 第1巻―南伊勢の歴史と伝承―』三重県郷土資料叢書第61集、三重県郷土史料刊行会、昭和58th year 8月10日、276p. * 式内社研究会 編『式内社調査報告 第六巻 東海道1』[[:en:Kogakkan University|Kogakkan University]]出版部、平成2nd year 2月28日、690p. ISBN 4-87644-080-8 * 三重県環境生活部文化振興課『熊野脇道 三重県 みえ歴史街道ウォーキングマップ』三重県環境生活部文化振興課、2012nd year * 『日本人なら知っておきたい 伊勢神宮と125の社』タツミムック、[[辰巳出版]]、2013rd year 11月1日、127p. ISBN 978-4-7778-1197-7 == See Also == * {{ill|List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|ja|伊勢国の式内社一覧|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|qq=}} * {{ill|List of 125 Shrines of Ise|ja|神宮125社の一覧|lt=List of 125 Shrines of Ise|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|3=伊勢国の式内社一覧|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of 125 Shrines of Ise|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|qq=}} * [[下外城田村]]

Worshipped as the [[Ubusunagami]] (guardian deity) of the region,<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk"/> she is classified as a [[Kunitsukami]] (earthly deity).<ref name="tm"/> She is revered as the protector of rice field irrigation, sacred fields, and Narahara.<ref name="tm"/> According to tradition, she dedicated a sacred area to protect [[Yamato-hime-no-mikoto]] during her arduous journey.<ref name="tm"/>

There is debate regarding the original shrine site, as the description in the *Kōtaijingu Gishiki-chō* does not align with the current location.<ref name="sk7"/> Some theories suggest the original site was deep in Miyako's mountains,<ref>Shikinaisha Research Association, 1990, pp. 237–238.</ref> while others propose it was at **Jōtō Shrine**.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Course 18: Exploring Satoyama Shrines|url=http://www.isekanko.jp/k-cycling/course18.html|publisher=Ise City Tourism Association|accessdate=2014-06-01}}</ref> Despite these theories, the shrine has remained at its current site since its restoration.<ref name="sk7"/>
The current shrine pavilion was rebuilt on October 8, 1980.<ref name="sk9"/>
== Rituals == Although there is no resident [[Kannushi]] (Shinto priest), one **Ikanushi** is appointed to manage daily affairs.<ref name="sk89">Shikinaisha Research Association, 1990, pp. 238–239.</ref>
Key rituals include: - **Kinensai (February 19)**: Prayer for abundant harvest - **Tsukinamisai (June 19 & December 19)**: Monthly festival - **Kannamesai (October 19)**: Offering of the first fruits to the deity - **Niinamesai (November 25)**: Celebration of the new harvest
Additional remote rituals are performed at the **Gojō-den Hall** of the Inner Shrine for New Year’s Day, National Foundation Day (February 11), and other imperial-related festivals.<ref name="sk89"/>
== Surroundings == The shrine is located west of the Miyako settlement, adjacent to the Hirose Seikō Tamaki Factory. It can be accessed via a gravel path after descending a slope.<ref>Ise Culture Publishing, 2008, p. 57.</ref> Nearby are several [[kofun]] (ancient Kofuns).<ref>Mie Prefecture Culture Promotion Division, 2012, p. 8.</ref> * **JR Sangu Line - Taki Station**: Approximately 35-minute walk<ref name="tm"/>. * **Ise Expressway - Tamaki IC**: About 2 minutes (830 m) by car.
== References ==

ISBN 978-4-05-610047-1 * 金子延夫『玉城町史 第1巻―南伊勢の歴史と伝承―』三重県郷土資料叢書第61集、三重県郷土史料刊行会、昭和58th year 8月10日、276p. * 式内社研究会 編『式内社調査報告 第六巻 東海道1』[[:en:Kogakkan University|Kogakkan University]]出版部、平成2nd year 2月28日、690p. {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q17227047]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:28, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | Name = {{ill|Narahara Shrine|ja|奈良波良神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Narahara Shrine|qq=}} | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = File:Narahara-jinja.jpg | location = 833 Yagurado, Miyako, [[:en:Tamaki|Tamaki]], [[:en:Watarai District|Watarai District]], [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]], Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|34.471806|N|136.624861|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | Map = Japan Mie | deity = {{ill|Narahara-hime-no-mikoto|ja|那良原比女命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Narahara-hime-no-mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} | type = {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|qq=draft|lt=Shikinaisha}} (minor Rank), {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} auxiliary shrine (*sessha*) | Established = Before [[Enryaku era|Enryaku]] 23 (804 CE)<ref name="sk7">Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): p. 237.</ref> | Main Hall = [[Shinmei-zukuri]] | Other Names = | festival = [[Kannamesai Festival|Kannamesai]] (Harvest Festival) | Rituals = | location = {{ill|Tamaki|qid=Q587348}} | architecture_style = {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|qid=Q930545}} }}
'''{{ill|Narahara Shrine|ja|奈良波良神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Narahara Shrine|qq=}}''' (*Narahara Jinja*) is an auxiliary shrine (*sessha*) of {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (the Inner Shrine of the [[Ise Grand Shrine]]), located in [[:en:Tamaki|Tamaki]], [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]], Japan. It Ranks 22nd among the auxiliary shrines of the Inner Shrine.<ref>Ujiyamada City Office (1929): pp. 9–11.</ref>
The name "Narahara" means "oak plain," suggesting that the surrounding area was once a plain abundant with [[Nara tree|Nara]] (oak) trees.<ref name="ic">Ise Bunka Publishing (2008): p. 59.</ref><ref name="gk">Gakken Publishing (2013): p. 67.</ref>
== Overview == The shrine is located at Yagurado 833, Miyako, [[:en:Tamaki|Tamaki]], [[:en:Watarai District|Watarai District]], [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]], Japan.<ref name="tm">Tatsumi Publishing (2013): p. 48.</ref> It is situated on gently sloping terrain surrounded by privately owned forests to the east and west.<ref name="sk7"/> The shrine was established by [[:en:Yamatohime-no-mikoto|Yamatohime-no-mikoto]].<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk"/> The sacred object (*shintai*) of the shrine is a stone.<ref>Kaneko (1983): p. 59.</ref>
=== Shrine Name and location === Although officially named '''Narahara Shrine''' (*奈良波良神社*), it is referred to as '''Narahara Shrine''' (*楢原神社*) in the ''Ritual Records of the Imperial Grand Shrine'' (*皇太神宮儀式帳*).<ref name="sk7"/> The name suggests that the area was once covered with oak (*nara*) trees. The shrine is located in Miyako, which was the site of the "Miyako Misono," a sacred rice field (*shinden*) belonging to {{ill|Toyōke Daijingū|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyōke Daijingū|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (the Outer Shrine of Ise).<ref name="ic"/>
According to the ''Seiyō Gorin Ikkyō'' (*勢陽五鈴遺響*), the name "Miyako" (meaning "capital") derives from an ancient plan to relocate the capital to this area. However, this plan was abandoned due to the emergence of Mt. Daibutsu (*大仏山*), leaving only the name to reflect the intended capital.<ref name="sk7"/> While the ''Report on the Investigation of Shikinaisha'' dismisses this as a "baseless theory," it acknowledges the connection between the shrine's name "'''Nara'''hara" and "Miyako" (都, meaning "capital").<ref name="sk7"/> The Tamaki Town Office explains that the old spelling of Miyako was "Miyako" (*宮子*), meaning "child of the shrine," symbolizing the blessings received from the deities.<ref>{{Cite web|language=ja|url=http://www.tamaki-kanko.jp/html/special_jingu-yukari.html|title=巡る神宮ゆかりの地 (Exploring Places Connected to the Grand Shrine)|publisher=Tamaki Town Office, Industrial Promotion Division|work=Nohon Tamaki Tabi|accessdate=2014-06-01|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20140601033540/http://www.tamaki-kanko.jp/html/special_jingu-yukari.html|archivedate=2014-06-01}}</ref>
== Grounds == The shrine grounds cover an area of 9,500 square meters.<ref name="sk9">Shikinaisha Research Association, 1990, p. 239.</ref> In ancient times, the grounds spanned approximately 5 chō (≈49,585 m²).<ref name="sk9"/> Although the shrine forest was confiscated during the 1871 [[Uchichiryo Edict]], 6,807 m² were returned in 1892.<ref name="sk9"/> The sacred grove is mainly composed of [[:en:Hinoki cypress|Hinoki cypress]] trees, with some [[:en:Cedar|Cedar]] and [[:en:Pine|Pine]] mixed in.<ref name="sk9"/>
Within the grounds, there is a natural stone [[:en:Chōzuya|Chōzuya]] (water basin) donated by devotees.<ref name="ic"/> A stone monument erected by the [[:en:Kishu Domain|Kishu Domain]] prohibiting the killing of living beings remains on site.<ref name="gk"/>
The shrine pavilion, located near the center of the grounds, is constructed in the [[Shinmei-zukuri]] style with a [[:en:board roof|board roof]]. It measures approximately 1.5 meters in length, 1.1 meters in width, and 2.9 meters in height.<ref name="sk0">Shikinaisha Research Association, 1990, p. 240.</ref> It faces south and is enclosed by a single layer of [[:en:Tamagaki|Tamagaki]] fence with a [[Shinmei torii]] gate.<ref name="sk0"/>
== Enshrined deity == The enshrined deity is **Narahara Hime-no-Mikoto**.<ref name="ic"/> The deity is referred to as "Narawara Hime-no-Mikoto" in the *Ujiyamada City History*.<ref name="uy">Ujiyamada City Office, 1929, p. 11.</ref> She is considered the daughter of **Ōminakami-no-Mikoto**.<ref name="sk7"/>
Worshipped as the [[Ubusunagami]] (guardian deity) of the region,<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk"/> she is classified as a [[Kunitsukami]] (earthly deity).<ref name="tm"/> She is revered as the protector of rice field irrigation, sacred fields, and Narahara.<ref name="tm"/> According to tradition, she dedicated a sacred area to protect [[Yamato-hime-no-mikoto]] during her arduous journey.<ref name="tm"/>
== History == The shrine is believed to have been founded by Yamato-hime-no-mikoto, as mentioned in the *Kōtaijingu Gishiki-chō*.<ref name="sk7"/> Since this document dates back to 804 (Enryaku 23), the shrine's existence predates this period.<ref name="sk7"/> During the [[:en:Medieval period|Medieval period]], the shrine fell into ruin until it was restored in 1663 (Kanbun 3) through the efforts of **Ōnakatomi Seichō (Kawabe Seichō)**.<ref>Shikinaisha Research Association, 1990, pp. 237, 240.</ref>
There is debate regarding the original shrine site, as the description in the *Kōtaijingu Gishiki-chō* does not align with the current location.<ref name="sk7"/> Some theories suggest the original site was deep in Miyako's mountains,<ref>Shikinaisha Research Association, 1990, pp. 237–238.</ref> while others propose it was at **Jōtō Shrine**.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Course 18: Exploring Satoyama Shrines|url=http://www.isekanko.jp/k-cycling/course18.html|publisher=Ise City Tourism Association|accessdate=2014-06-01}}</ref> Despite these theories, the shrine has remained at its current site since its restoration.<ref name="sk7"/>
The current shrine pavilion was rebuilt on October 8, 1980.<ref name="sk9"/>
== Rituals == Although there is no resident [[Kannushi]] (Shinto priest), one **Ikanushi** is appointed to manage daily affairs.<ref name="sk89">Shikinaisha Research Association, 1990, pp. 238–239.</ref>
Key rituals include: - **Kinensai (February 19)**: Prayer for abundant harvest - **Tsukinamisai (June 19 & December 19)**: Monthly festival - **Kannamesai (October 19)**: Offering of the first fruits to the deity - **Niinamesai (November 25)**: Celebration of the new harvest
Additional remote rituals are performed at the **Gojō-den Hall** of the Inner Shrine for New Year’s Day, National Foundation Day (February 11), and other imperial-related festivals.<ref name="sk89"/>
== Surroundings == The shrine is located west of the Miyako settlement, adjacent to the Hirose Seikō Tamaki Factory. It can be accessed via a gravel path after descending a slope.<ref>Ise Culture Publishing, 2008, p. 57.</ref> Nearby are several [[kofun]] (ancient Kofuns).<ref>Mie Prefecture Culture Promotion Division, 2012, p. 8.</ref> * **JR Sangu Line - Taki Station**: Approximately 35-minute walk<ref name="tm"/>. * **Ise Expressway - Tamaki IC**: About 2 minutes (830 m) by car.
== References ==

ISBN 978-4-900759-37-4 * 宇治山田市役所 編『宇治山田市史 上巻』宇治山田市役所、昭和4th year 1月20日、862p. * [[:en:学研パブリッシング|学研パブリッシング]]『伊勢神宮に行こう』Gakken Mook神社紀行セレクションvol.1、[[薗田稔]]監修、学研マーケティング、2013rd year 7月4日、82p. ISBN 978-4-05-610047-1 * 金子延夫『玉城町史 第1巻―南伊勢の歴史と伝承―』三重県郷土資料叢書第61集、三重県郷土史料刊行会、昭和58th year 8月10日、276p. * 式内社研究会 編『式内社調査報告 第六巻 東海道1』[[:en:Kogakkan University|Kogakkan University]]出版部、平成2nd year 2月28日、690p. ISBN 978-4-7778-1197-7 == See Also == * {{ill|List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|ja|伊勢国の式内社一覧|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|qq=}} * {{ill|List of 125 Shrines of Ise|ja|神宮125社の一覧|lt=List of 125 Shrines of Ise|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|3=伊勢国の式内社一覧|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of 125 Shrines of Ise|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|qq=}} * [[下外城田村]]

According to the ''Seiyō Gorin Ikkyō'' (*勢陽五鈴遺響*), the name "Miyako" (meaning "capital") derives from an ancient plan to relocate the capital to this area. However, this plan was abandoned due to the emergence of Mt. Daibutsu (*大仏山*), leaving only the name to reflect the intended capital.<ref name="sk7"/> While the ''Report on the Investigation of Shikinaisha'' dismisses this as a "baseless theory," it acknowledges the connection between the shrine's name "'''Nara'''hara" and "Miyako" (都, meaning "capital").<ref name="sk7"/> The Tamaki Town Office explains that the old spelling of Miyako was "Miyako" (*宮子*), meaning "child of the shrine," symbolizing the blessings received from the deities.<ref>{{Cite web|language=ja|url=http://www.tamaki-kanko.jp/html/special_jingu-yukari.html|title=巡る神宮ゆかりの地 (Exploring Places Connected to the Grand Shrine)|publisher=Tamaki Town Office, Industrial Promotion Division|work=Nohon Tamaki Tabi|accessdate=2014-06-01|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20140601033540/http://www.tamaki-kanko.jp/html/special_jingu-yukari.html|archivedate=2014-06-01}}</ref>
== Grounds == The shrine grounds cover an area of 9,500 square meters.<ref name="sk9">Shikinaisha Research Association, 1990, p. 239.</ref> In ancient times, the grounds spanned approximately 5 chō (≈49,585 m²).<ref name="sk9"/> Although the shrine forest was confiscated during the 1871 [[Uchichiryo Edict]], 6,807 m² were returned in 1892.<ref name="sk9"/> The sacred grove is mainly composed of [[:en:Hinoki cypress|Hinoki cypress]] trees, with some [[:en:Cedar|Cedar]] and [[:en:Pine|Pine]] mixed in.<ref name="sk9"/>
Within the grounds, there is a natural stone [[:en:Chōzuya|Chōzuya]] (water basin) donated by devotees.<ref name="ic"/> A stone monument erected by the [[:en:Kishu Domain|Kishu Domain]] prohibiting the killing of living beings remains on site.<ref name="gk"/>
The shrine pavilion, located near the center of the grounds, is constructed in the [[Shinmei-zukuri]] style with a [[:en:board roof|board roof]]. It measures approximately 1.5 meters in length, 1.1 meters in width, and 2.9 meters in height.<ref name="sk0">Shikinaisha Research Association, 1990, p. 240.</ref> It faces south and is enclosed by a single layer of [[:en:Tamagaki|Tamagaki]] fence with a [[Shinmei torii]] gate.<ref name="sk0"/>
== Enshrined deity == The enshrined deity is **Narahara Hime-no-Mikoto**.<ref name="ic"/> The deity is referred to as "Narawara Hime-no-Mikoto" in the *Ujiyamada City History*.<ref name="uy">Ujiyamada City Office, 1929, p. 11.</ref> She is considered the daughter of **Ōminakami-no-Mikoto**.<ref name="sk7"/>
Worshipped as the [[Ubusunagami]] (guardian deity) of the region,<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk"/> she is classified as a [[Kunitsukami]] (earthly deity).<ref name="tm"/> She is revered as the protector of rice field irrigation, sacred fields, and Narahara.<ref name="tm"/> According to tradition, she dedicated a sacred area to protect [[Yamato-hime-no-mikoto]] during her arduous journey.<ref name="tm"/>
== History == The shrine is believed to have been founded by Yamato-hime-no-mikoto, as mentioned in the *Kōtaijingu Gishiki-chō*.<ref name="sk7"/> Since this document dates back to 804 (Enryaku 23), the shrine's existence predates this period.<ref name="sk7"/> During the [[:en:Medieval period|Medieval period]], the shrine fell into ruin until it was restored in 1663 (Kanbun 3) through the efforts of **Ōnakatomi Seichō (Kawabe Seichō)**.<ref>Shikinaisha Research Association, 1990, pp. 237, 240.</ref>
There is debate regarding the original shrine site, as the description in the *Kōtaijingu Gishiki-chō* does not align with the current location.<ref name="sk7"/> Some theories suggest the original site was deep in Miyako's mountains,<ref>Shikinaisha Research Association, 1990, pp. 237–238.</ref> while others propose it was at **Jōtō Shrine**.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Course 18: Exploring Satoyama Shrines|url=http://www.isekanko.jp/k-cycling/course18.html|publisher=Ise City Tourism Association|accessdate=2014-06-01}}</ref> Despite these theories, the shrine has remained at its current site since its restoration.<ref name="sk7"/>
The current shrine pavilion was rebuilt on October 8, 1980.<ref name="sk9"/>
== Rituals == Although there is no resident [[Kannushi]] (Shinto priest), one **Ikanushi** is appointed to manage daily affairs.<ref name="sk89">Shikinaisha Research Association, 1990, pp. 238–239.</ref>
Key rituals include: - **Kinensai (February 19)**: Prayer for abundant harvest - **Tsukinamisai (June 19 & December 19)**: Monthly festival - **Kannamesai (October 19)**: Offering of the first fruits to the deity - **Niinamesai (November 25)**: Celebration of the new harvest
Additional remote rituals are performed at the **Gojō-den Hall** of the Inner Shrine for New Year’s Day, National Foundation Day (February 11), and other imperial-related festivals.<ref name="sk89"/>
== Surroundings == The shrine is located west of the Miyako settlement, adjacent to the Hirose Seikō Tamaki Factory. It can be accessed via a gravel path after descending a slope.<ref>Ise Culture Publishing, 2008, p. 57.</ref> Nearby are several [[kofun]] (ancient Kofuns).<ref>Mie Prefecture Culture Promotion Division, 2012, p. 8.</ref> * **JR Sangu Line - Taki Station**: Approximately 35-minute walk<ref name="tm"/>. * **Ise Expressway - Tamaki IC**: About 2 minutes (830 m) by car.
== References ==
{{Reflist|2}}

ISBN 978-4-900759-37-4 * 宇治山田市役所 編『宇治山田市史 上巻』宇治山田市役所、昭和4th year 1月20日、862p. * [[:en:学研パブリッシング|学研パブリッシング]]『伊勢神宮に行こう』Gakken Mook神社紀行セレクションvol.1、[[薗田稔]]監修、学研マーケティング、2013rd year 7月4日、82p. ISBN 978-4-05-610047-1 * 金子延夫『玉城町史 第1巻―南伊勢の歴史と伝承―』三重県郷土資料叢書第61集、三重県郷土史料刊行会、昭和58th year 8月10日、276p. * 式内社研究会 編『式内社調査報告 第六巻 東海道1』[[:en:Kogakkan University|Kogakkan University]]出版部、平成2nd year 2月28日、690p. ISBN 4-87644-080-8 * 三重県環境生活部文化振興課『熊野脇道 三重県 みえ歴史街道ウォーキングマップ』三重県環境生活部文化振興課、2012nd year * 『日本人なら知っておきたい 伊勢神宮と125の社』タツミムック、[[辰巳出版]]、2013rd year 11月1日、127p. ISBN 978-4-7778-1197-7 == See Also == * {{ill|List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|ja|伊勢国の式内社一覧|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|qq=}} * {{ill|List of 125 Shrines of Ise|ja|神宮125社の一覧|lt=List of 125 Shrines of Ise|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|3=伊勢国の式内社一覧|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of 125 Shrines of Ise|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|qq=}} * [[下外城田村]]

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q17227047]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:28, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | Name = {{ill|Narahara Shrine|ja|奈良波良神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Narahara Shrine|qq=}} | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = File:Narahara-jinja.jpg | location = 833 Yagurado, Miyako, [[:en:Tamaki|Tamaki]], [[:en:Watarai District|Watarai District]], [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]], Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|34.471806|N|136.624861|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | Map = Japan Mie | deity = {{ill|Narahara-hime-no-mikoto|ja|那良原比女命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Narahara-hime-no-mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} | type = {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|qq=draft|lt=Shikinaisha}} (minor Rank), {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} auxiliary shrine (*sessha*) | Established = Before [[Enryaku era|Enryaku]] 23 (804 CE)<ref name="sk7">Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): p. 237.</ref> | Main Hall = [[Shinmei-zukuri]] | Other Names = | festival = [[Kannamesai Festival|Kannamesai]] (Harvest Festival) | Rituals = | location = {{ill|Tamaki|qid=Q587348}} | architecture_style = {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|qid=Q930545}} }}
'''{{ill|Narahara Shrine|ja|奈良波良神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Narahara Shrine|qq=}}''' (*Narahara Jinja*) is an auxiliary shrine (*sessha*) of {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (the Inner Shrine of the [[Ise Grand Shrine]]), located in [[:en:Tamaki|Tamaki]], [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]], Japan. It Ranks 22nd among the auxiliary shrines of the Inner Shrine.<ref>Ujiyamada City Office (1929): pp. 9–11.</ref>
The name "Narahara" means "oak plain," suggesting that the surrounding area was once a plain abundant with [[Nara tree|Nara]] (oak) trees.<ref name="ic">Ise Bunka Publishing (2008): p. 59.</ref><ref name="gk">Gakken Publishing (2013): p. 67.</ref>
== Overview == The shrine is located at Yagurado 833, Miyako, [[:en:Tamaki|Tamaki]], [[:en:Watarai District|Watarai District]], [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]], Japan.<ref name="tm">Tatsumi Publishing (2013): p. 48.</ref> It is situated on gently sloping terrain surrounded by privately owned forests to the east and west.<ref name="sk7"/> The shrine was established by [[:en:Yamatohime-no-mikoto|Yamatohime-no-mikoto]].<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk"/> The sacred object (*shintai*) of the shrine is a stone.<ref>Kaneko (1983): p. 59.</ref>
=== Shrine Name and location === Although officially named '''Narahara Shrine''' (*奈良波良神社*), it is referred to as '''Narahara Shrine''' (*楢原神社*) in the ''Ritual Records of the Imperial Grand Shrine'' (*皇太神宮儀式帳*).<ref name="sk7"/> The name suggests that the area was once covered with oak (*nara*) trees. The shrine is located in Miyako, which was the site of the "Miyako Misono," a sacred rice field (*shinden*) belonging to {{ill|Toyōke Daijingū|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyōke Daijingū|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (the Outer Shrine of Ise).<ref name="ic"/>
According to the ''Seiyō Gorin Ikkyō'' (*勢陽五鈴遺響*), the name "Miyako" (meaning "capital") derives from an ancient plan to relocate the capital to this area. However, this plan was abandoned due to the emergence of Mt. Daibutsu (*大仏山*), leaving only the name to reflect the intended capital.<ref name="sk7"/> While the ''Report on the Investigation of Shikinaisha'' dismisses this as a "baseless theory," it acknowledges the connection between the shrine's name "'''Nara'''hara" and "Miyako" (都, meaning "capital").<ref name="sk7"/> The Tamaki Town Office explains that the old spelling of Miyako was "Miyako" (*宮子*), meaning "child of the shrine," symbolizing the blessings received from the deities.<ref>{{Cite web|language=ja|url=http://www.tamaki-kanko.jp/html/special_jingu-yukari.html|title=巡る神宮ゆかりの地 (Exploring Places Connected to the Grand Shrine)|publisher=Tamaki Town Office, Industrial Promotion Division|work=Nohon Tamaki Tabi|accessdate=2014-06-01|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20140601033540/http://www.tamaki-kanko.jp/html/special_jingu-yukari.html|archivedate=2014-06-01}}</ref>
== Grounds == The shrine grounds cover an area of 9,500 square meters.<ref name="sk9">Shikinaisha Research Association, 1990, p. 239.</ref> In ancient times, the grounds spanned approximately 5 chō (≈49,585 m²).<ref name="sk9"/> Although the shrine forest was confiscated during the 1871 [[Uchichiryo Edict]], 6,807 m² were returned in 1892.<ref name="sk9"/> The sacred grove is mainly composed of [[:en:Hinoki cypress|Hinoki cypress]] trees, with some [[:en:Cedar|Cedar]] and [[:en:Pine|Pine]] mixed in.<ref name="sk9"/>
Within the grounds, there is a natural stone [[:en:Chōzuya|Chōzuya]] (water basin) donated by devotees.<ref name="ic"/> A stone monument erected by the [[:en:Kishu Domain|Kishu Domain]] prohibiting the killing of living beings remains on site.<ref name="gk"/>
The shrine pavilion, located near the center of the grounds, is constructed in the [[Shinmei-zukuri]] style with a [[:en:board roof|board roof]]. It measures approximately 1.5 meters in length, 1.1 meters in width, and 2.9 meters in height.<ref name="sk0">Shikinaisha Research Association, 1990, p. 240.</ref> It faces south and is enclosed by a single layer of [[:en:Tamagaki|Tamagaki]] fence with a [[Shinmei torii]] gate.<ref name="sk0"/>
== Enshrined deity == The enshrined deity is **Narahara Hime-no-Mikoto**.<ref name="ic"/> The deity is referred to as "Narawara Hime-no-Mikoto" in the *Ujiyamada City History*.<ref name="uy">Ujiyamada City Office, 1929, p. 11.</ref> She is considered the daughter of **Ōminakami-no-Mikoto**.<ref name="sk7"/>
Worshipped as the [[Ubusunagami]] (guardian deity) of the region,<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk"/> she is classified as a [[Kunitsukami]] (earthly deity).<ref name="tm"/> She is revered as the protector of rice field irrigation, sacred fields, and Narahara.<ref name="tm"/> According to tradition, she dedicated a sacred area to protect [[Yamato-hime-no-mikoto]] during her arduous journey.<ref name="tm"/>
== History == The shrine is believed to have been founded by Yamato-hime-no-mikoto, as mentioned in the *Kōtaijingu Gishiki-chō*.<ref name="sk7"/> Since this document dates back to 804 (Enryaku 23), the shrine's existence predates this period.<ref name="sk7"/> During the [[:en:Medieval period|Medieval period]], the shrine fell into ruin until it was restored in 1663 (Kanbun 3) through the efforts of **Ōnakatomi Seichō (Kawabe Seichō)**.<ref>Shikinaisha Research Association, 1990, pp. 237, 240.</ref>
There is debate regarding the original shrine site, as the description in the *Kōtaijingu Gishiki-chō* does not align with the current location.<ref name="sk7"/> Some theories suggest the original site was deep in Miyako's mountains,<ref>Shikinaisha Research Association, 1990, pp. 237–238.</ref> while others propose it was at **Jōtō Shrine**.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Course 18: Exploring Satoyama Shrines|url=http://www.isekanko.jp/k-cycling/course18.html|publisher=Ise City Tourism Association|accessdate=2014-06-01}}</ref> Despite these theories, the shrine has remained at its current site since its restoration.<ref name="sk7"/>
The current shrine pavilion was rebuilt on October 8, 1980.<ref name="sk9"/>
== Rituals == Although there is no resident [[Kannushi]] (Shinto priest), one **Ikanushi** is appointed to manage daily affairs.<ref name="sk89">Shikinaisha Research Association, 1990, pp. 238–239.</ref>
Key rituals include: - **Kinensai (February 19)**: Prayer for abundant harvest - **Tsukinamisai (June 19 & December 19)**: Monthly festival - **Kannamesai (October 19)**: Offering of the first fruits to the deity - **Niinamesai (November 25)**: Celebration of the new harvest
Additional remote rituals are performed at the **Gojō-den Hall** of the Inner Shrine for New Year’s Day, National Foundation Day (February 11), and other imperial-related festivals.<ref name="sk89"/>
== Surroundings == The shrine is located west of the Miyako settlement, adjacent to the Hirose Seikō Tamaki Factory. It can be accessed via a gravel path after descending a slope.<ref>Ise Culture Publishing, 2008, p. 57.</ref> Nearby are several [[kofun]] (ancient Kofuns).<ref>Mie Prefecture Culture Promotion Division, 2012, p. 8.</ref> * **JR Sangu Line - Taki Station**: Approximately 35-minute walk<ref name="tm"/>. * **Ise Expressway - Tamaki IC**: About 2 minutes (830 m) by car.
